**Anglicanism**, also known as **Episcopalianism** in some countries,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [Western](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") [Christian tradition](/wiki/Christian_tradition "Christian tradition") which developed from the practices, [liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy"), and identity of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") following the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"),[[3]](#cite_note-cofe-3) in the context of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in Europe. It is one of the largest branches of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), with around 110 million adherents within the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), and more than 400,000 outside of the Anglican Communion, worldwide as of 2025.[[4]](#cite_note-McKeown-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

Adherents of Anglicanism are called *Anglicans*; they are also called *Episcopalians*[[8]](#cite_note-8) in some countries. Most are members of national or regional [ecclesiastical provinces](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province#Anglican_Communion "Ecclesiastical province") of the international Anglican Communion,[[9]](#cite_note-acomm-9) one of the largest Christian bodies in the world,[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorsley2015-10) and the world's third-largest Christian communion.[[11]](#cite_note-ChristianTrends1-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[4]](#cite_note-McKeown-4)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) The provinces within the Anglican Communion have historically been in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [See of Canterbury](/wiki/See_of_Canterbury "See of Canterbury") and thus with the [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), whom the communion refers to as its *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares#Anglican_Communion "Primus inter pares")* ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), 'first among equals'). The archbishop calls the decennial [Lambeth Conference](/wiki/Lambeth_Conference "Lambeth Conference"), chairs the meeting of [primates](/wiki/Primates_in_the_Anglican_Communion "Primates in the Anglican Communion"), and is the president of the [Anglican Consultative Council](/wiki/Anglican_Consultative_Council "Anglican Consultative Council").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-ODCC-20) Some churches that are not part of the Anglican Communion or recognised by it also call themselves Anglican, including those that are within the [Continuing Anglican movement](/wiki/Continuing_Anglican_movement "Continuing Anglican movement") and [Anglican realignment](/wiki/Anglican_realignment "Anglican realignment").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPercy2005217-21)

Anglicans base their Christian faith on the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), traditions of the apostolic church, [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") ("[historic episcopate](/wiki/Historic_episcopate_(Anglican_views) "Historic episcopate (Anglican views)")"), and the writings of the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"), as well as historically, the *[Thirty-nine Articles](/wiki/Thirty-nine_Articles "Thirty-nine Articles")* and *[The Books of Homilies](/wiki/The_Books_of_Homilies "The Books of Homilies")*.[[22]](#cite_note-Samuel2020-22)[[3]](#cite_note-cofe-3)

Anglicanism forms a branch of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), having declared its independence from the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") through the [Act of Supremacy](/wiki/Acts_of_Supremacy "Acts of Supremacy") in 1534, a separation later consolidated by the [Elizabethan Religious Settlement](/wiki/Elizabethan_Religious_Settlement "Elizabethan Religious Settlement"). Many of the Anglican formularies of the mid-16th century correspond closely to those of historical [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch1996617-23) In the first half of the 17th century, the Church of England and the associated [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") were presented by some Anglican [divines](#divines) as comprising a distinct Christian tradition, with theologies, structures, and forms of worship representing a different kind of middle way, or *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")*, originally between Lutheranism and Calvinism,[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch1996179-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) and later between Protestantism and Catholicism – a perspective that came to be highly influential in later theories of Anglican identity and expressed in the description of Anglicanism as "catholic and reformed".[[26]](#cite_note-26) The degree of distinction between Protestant and Catholic tendencies within Anglicanism is routinely a matter of debate both within specific Anglican churches and the Anglican Communion. The *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* is unique to Anglicanism, the collection of services in one prayer book used for centuries. The book is acknowledged as a principal tie that binds the Anglican Communion as a liturgical tradition.[[22]](#cite_note-Samuel2020-22)

After the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), Anglican congregations in the United States and [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") (which would later form the basis for the modern country of Canada) were each reconstituted into autonomous churches with their own bishops and self-governing structures; these were known as the [Protestant Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") in the United States and the [Church of England in the Dominion of Canada](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Anglican Church of Canada"). Through the expansion of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and the activity of [Christian missions](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission"), this model was adopted as the model for many newly formed churches, especially in Africa, [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia"), and the Asia-Pacific. In the late-19th century, the term *Anglicanism* was coined to describe the common religious tradition of these churches and also that of the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church"), which, though originating earlier within the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), had come to be recognised as sharing this common identity. By the 21st century, the global centre of Anglicanism had shifted to the [Global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South"), particularly sub-Saharan Africa, with 63,556,000 in Africa, 24,400,000 in Europe, 4,565,000 in Oceania, 2,689,000 in Northern America, 1,230,000 in Asia, and 959,000 in Latin America in 2020.[[27]](#cite_note-:3-27)[[28]](#cite_note-:4-28)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Rochester_cathedral_stained_glass_2.jpg/250px-Rochester_cathedral_stained_glass_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochester_cathedral_stained_glass_2.jpg)

Jesus supporting an English flag and staff in the crook of his right arm depicted in a stained glass window in [Rochester Cathedral](/wiki/Rochester_Cathedral "Rochester Cathedral") in [Rochester, Kent](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent"), England

The word *Anglican* originates in *ecclesia anglicana*, a [medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") phrase that means the *English Church*.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoyes1907499–500-29)[[a]](#cite_note-31) Adherents of Anglicanism are called *Anglicans*. As an adjective, *Anglican* is used to describe the people, institutions, churches, liturgical traditions, and theological concepts developed by the Church of England.[[20]](#cite_note-ODCC-20)

As a noun, an Anglican is a church member in the Anglican Communion. The word is also used by followers of separated groups that have left the communion or have been founded separately from it. The word originally referred only to the teachings and rites of Christians throughout the world in communion with the [see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") of [Canterbury](/wiki/Diocese_of_Canterbury "Diocese of Canterbury") but has come to sometimes be extended to any church following those traditions or rites rather than actual membership in the Anglican Communion.[[20]](#cite_note-ODCC-20)

Although the term *Anglican* is found referring to the Church of England as far back as the 16th century, its use did not become general until the latter half of the 19th century. In British parliamentary legislation referring to the English [Established Church](/wiki/Established_Church "Established Church"), there is no need for a description; it is simply the Church of England, though the word *Protestant* is used in many legal acts specifying the succession to the Crown and qualifications for office. When the [Union with Ireland Act](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") created the United Church of England and Ireland, it is specified that it shall be one "Protestant Episcopal Church", thereby distinguishing its form of church government from the Presbyterian polity that prevails in the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland").[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The word *Episcopal* ("of or pertaining to bishops") is preferred in the title of the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") (the province of the Anglican Communion covering the United States) and the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church"), though the full name of the former is *The Protestant Episcopal Church in the United States of America*. Elsewhere, however, the term *Anglican Church* came to be preferred as it distinguished these churches from others that maintain an [episcopal polity](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity").

### Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

In its structures, theology, and forms of worship, Anglicanism emerged as a distinct Christian tradition representing a middle ground between [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") varieties of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism");[[32]](#cite_note-Anglican_and_Episcopal_History-33) after the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement"), Anglicanism has often been characterized as representing a *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")* ('middle way') between Protestantism as a whole, and Catholicism.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch1996179-24)

The faith of Anglicans is founded in the [Scriptures](/wiki/Bible "Bible") and the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel"), the traditions of the [Apostolic](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age") Church, the [historical episcopate](/wiki/Historical_episcopate "Historical episcopate"), the [first four ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council"),[[33]](#cite_note-Kasper2009-34) and the early [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"), especially those active during the five initial centuries of Christianity, according to the *quinquasaecularist* principle proposed by the English bishop [Lancelot Andrewes](/wiki/Lancelot_Andrewes "Lancelot Andrewes") and the Lutheran dissident [Georg Calixtus](/wiki/Georg_Calixtus "Georg Calixtus").

Anglicans understand the [Old](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") and [New Testaments](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") as "containing all things necessary for salvation" and as being the rule and ultimate standard of faith.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaye199646–47-35) Reason and tradition are seen as valuable means to interpret scripture (a position first formulated in detail by [Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker "Richard Hooker")), but there is no full mutual agreement among Anglicans about *exactly how* scripture, reason, and tradition interact (or ought to interact) with each other.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaker1996113–115-36) Anglicans understand the [Apostles' Creed](/wiki/Apostles%27_Creed "Apostles' Creed") as the baptismal symbol and the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") as the sufficient [statement of the Christian faith](/wiki/Creed "Creed").

Anglicans believe the catholic and apostolic faith is revealed in [Holy Scripture](/wiki/Holy_Scripture "Holy Scripture") and the ecumenical creeds (Apostles', Nicene and Athanasian) and interpret these in light of the Christian tradition of the historic church, scholarship, reason, and experience.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Anglicans celebrate the traditional sacraments, with special emphasis being given to the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), also called Holy Communion, the Lord's Supper, or the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)"). The Eucharist is central to worship for most Anglicans as a communal offering of prayer and praise in which the life, death and resurrection of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") are proclaimed through prayer, reading of the Bible, singing, giving God thanks over the bread and wine for the innumerable benefits obtained through the passion of Christ; the breaking of the bread, the blessing of the cup, and the partaking of the body and blood of Christ as instituted at the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper"). The consecrated bread and wine are considered by Anglican formularies to be the true body and blood of Christ received in a spiritual manner. The sacrament is understood as being an outward and visible sign of an inward and spiritual grace given by Christ which conveys forgiveness and cleansing from sin. While many Anglicans celebrate the Eucharist in ways similar to the predominant [Latin Catholic](/wiki/Latin_Catholic "Latin Catholic") tradition, a considerable degree of liturgical freedom is permitted and worship styles range from simple to elaborate.

Unique to Anglicanism is the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* (BCP), the collection of services which worshippers in most Anglican churches have used for centuries. It was called *common prayer* originally because it was intended for use in all Church of England churches, which had previously followed differing local liturgies. The term was kept when the church became international because all Anglicans used to share in its use around the world. In 1549, the first *Book of Common Prayer* was compiled by [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), the then [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"). While it has since undergone many revisions and Anglican churches in different countries have developed other service books, the BCP is still acknowledged as one of the ties that bind Anglicans together.

## Identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Identity")]

See also: [History of the Anglican Communion](/wiki/History_of_the_Anglican_Communion "History of the Anglican Communion")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Saint_Alban_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Saint_Alban_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Alban_(cropped).jpg)

[Saint Alban](/wiki/Saint_Alban "Saint Alban") is venerated as the first-recorded British Christian martyr

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Augustinus_von_Canterbury.jpg/250px-Augustinus_von_Canterbury.jpg)](/wiki/File:Augustinus_von_Canterbury.jpg)

[Augustine of Canterbury](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury"), the first [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury")

According to legend, the founding of Christianity in Britain is commonly attributed to [Joseph of Arimathea](/wiki/Joseph_of_Arimathea "Joseph of Arimathea") and is commemorated at [Glastonbury Abbey](/wiki/Glastonbury_Abbey "Glastonbury Abbey").[[b]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBays201225Godfrey19629Kelly1999-40) Many of the early [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") wrote of the presence of Christianity in [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), with [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian") stating "those parts of Britain into which the Roman arms had never penetrated were become subject to Christ".[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBays201225Kelly1999Timpson184712-41) [Saint Alban](/wiki/Saint_Alban "Saint Alban"), who was executed in AD 209, is the first [Christian martyr](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr") in the British Isles. For this reason he is [venerated](/wiki/Venerated "Venerated") as the British [protomartyr](/wiki/Protomartyr "Protomartyr").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmentroutSlocum2000Bays201225CrossLivingstone2005-42) The historian [Heinrich Zimmer](/wiki/Heinrich_Zimmer_(Celticist) "Heinrich Zimmer (Celticist)") writes that "Just as Britain was a part of the Roman Empire, so the British Church formed (during the fourth century) a branch of the Catholic Church of the West; and during the whole of that century, from the [Council of Arles](/wiki/Synod_of_Arles "Synod of Arles") (316) onward, took part in all proceedings concerning the Church."[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1902107–109-43)

After [Roman troops withdrew from Britain](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain"), the "absence of Roman military and governmental influence and overall decline of Roman imperial political power enabled Britain and the surrounding isles to develop distinctively from the rest of the West. A new culture emerged around the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") among the [Celtic peoples](/wiki/Celts "Celts") with [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") at its core. What resulted was a form of Christianity distinct from Rome in many traditions and practices."[[c]](#cite_note-46)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHogue2010160-47)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHexhamRostMorehead200448De_Waal199852-48)

The historian [Charles Thomas](/wiki/Charles_Thomas_(historian) "Charles Thomas (historian)"), in addition to the [Celticist](/wiki/Celtic_studies "Celtic studies") Heinrich Zimmer, writes that the distinction between sub-Roman and post-Roman Insular Christianity, also known as Celtic Christianity, began to become apparent around AD 475,[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1981348Zimmer1902107–109-49) with the [Celtic churches](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") allowing married clergy,[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGodfrey1962440–441-50) observing [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") and Easter according to their own calendar,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoenig20007-51)[[49]](#cite_note-Cm-52) and having a different [tonsure](/wiki/Tonsure "Tonsure"); moreover, like the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and the [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") churches, the Celtic churches operated independently of the Pope's authority,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECairns1996172Grafton191169Hunter_Blair2003129-53) as a result of their isolated development in the British Isles.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter_Blair2003129Taylor191659-54)

In what is known as the [Gregorian mission](/wiki/Gregorian_mission "Gregorian mission"), [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I") sent [Augustine of Canterbury](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury") to the British Isles in AD 596, with the purpose of [evangelising](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism") the pagans there (who were largely [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_paganism "Anglo-Saxon paganism")),[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWright200825-55) as well as to reconcile the Celtic churches in the British Isles to the [See of Rome](/wiki/See_of_Rome "See of Rome").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoenig20007Wallace20099Wilken2012274–275-56) In [Kent](/wiki/Kent,_England "Kent, England"), Augustine persuaded the Anglo-Saxon king "[Æthelberht](/wiki/%C3%86thelberht_of_Kent "Æthelberht of Kent") and his people to accept Christianity".[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter200394-57) Augustine, on two occasions, "met in conference with members of the Celtic episcopacy, but no understanding was reached between them".[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter_Blair1966226-58)

Eventually, the "Christian Church of the Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Northumbria convened the [Synod of Whitby](/wiki/Synod_of_Whitby "Synod of Whitby") in 663/664 to decide whether to follow Celtic or Roman usages". This meeting, with [King Oswiu](/wiki/Oswiu "Oswiu") as the final decision maker, "led to the acceptance of Roman usage elsewhere in England and brought the English Church into close contact with the Continent".[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell2011112-59) As a result of assuming Roman usages, the Celtic Church surrendered its independence, and, from this point on, the Church in England "was no longer purely Celtic, but became Anglo-Roman-Celtic".[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardinge1995xii-60) The theologian Christopher L. Webber writes that "Although "the Roman form of Christianity became the dominant influence in Britain as in all of western Europe, Anglican Christianity has continued to have a distinctive quality because of its Celtic heritage."[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWebber1999-61)[[59]](#cite_note-62)[[60]](#cite_note-63)

Following the Synod of Whitby, tensions between Rome and the English king would gradually escalate, due in part to royal assertions that it was the custom of England for the king to exercise authority over the Church. In the late 1000s, [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") (William I) refused to swear fealty to the Pope citing English tradition,[[61]](#cite_note-64) controlled appointments to ecclesiastical offices[[62]](#cite_note-65) (a power historically reserved to the Pope) and forbade papal legates to enter England without royal permission.[[63]](#cite_note-66) In 1164, under [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England"), the [Constitutions of Clarendon](/wiki/Constitutions_of_Clarendon "Constitutions of Clarendon"), citing English custom, required royal assent for excommunications and mandated that ecclesiastical court appeals terminate with the king rather than the Pope.[[64]](#cite_note-67) The [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") in 1215, asserting that "the English Church shall be free, and shall have its rights undiminished, and its liberties unimpaired"[[65]](#cite_note-68) would be annulled by [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III"),[[66]](#cite_note-69) but reissued in both 1216 and 1225. Under [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"), in 1279, the [Statute of Mortmain](/wiki/Statutes_of_Mortmain "Statutes of Mortmain") required royal approval to grant or transfer land to the Church.[[67]](#cite_note-70) Additionally, Edward I would reject [Pope Boniface VIII](/wiki/Pope_Boniface_VIII "Pope Boniface VIII")'s bull *[Clericis Laicos](/wiki/Clericis_laicos "Clericis laicos")* which forbade secular taxation of clergy. Per the king's orders, non-compliant clergy were punished by law and church property was seized.[[68]](#cite_note-71) The 1351 [Statute of Provisors](/wiki/Statute_of_Provisors "Statute of Provisors"), under [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III"), prohibited papal appointments to English benefices, reserving the power for the king.[[69]](#cite_note-72) The 1353 [Statute of Praemunire](/wiki/Statute_of_Praemunire "Statute of Praemunire") prohibited appeals to papal courts for either ecclesiastical or temporal matters.[[70]](#cite_note-73) To date, neither the Statute of Provisors nor the Statute of Praemunire has been repealed. In 1401, [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_England "Henry IV of England")'s statute *[De Heretico Comburendo](/wiki/De_heretico_comburendo "De heretico comburendo")* would transfer heresy trials from ecclesiastical to secular courts, further cementing the tradition of English kings claiming authority over English ecclesiastical matters.[[71]](#cite_note-74) English delegates to the Councils of [Pisa](/wiki/Council_of_Pisa "Council of Pisa") (1409), [Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance") (1414–1418), and [Basel](/wiki/Council_Of_Basel "Council Of Basel") (1431–1445) would voice support for conciliarism in an attempt to limit the powers of the Pope over-against the bishops of the Church.[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76)

The Church in England remained united with Rome until the English Parliament passed the [Act of Supremacy](/wiki/Acts_of_Supremacy "Acts of Supremacy") in 1534, declaring King [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") the "Supreme Head" of the Church of England. This act culminated centuries of English monarchs asserting authority over ecclesiastical matters, from William I's refusal of papal fealty (1070s) to the Statutes of Provisors (1351) and Praemunire (1353). Henry and his theologians, including [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), cited these historical customs to justify royal supremacy, arguing that the crown traditionally governed the Church.[[74]](#cite_note-77) The immediate catalyst was Henry's need to annul his 24-year marriage to [Catherine of Aragon](/wiki/Catherine_of_Aragon "Catherine of Aragon"), which he believed was invalid based on biblical prohibitions (Leviticus 20:21) and the lack of a male heir, seen as divine judgment. When [Pope Clement VII](/wiki/Pope_Clement_VII "Pope Clement VII"), under pressure from [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"), refused the annulment, Henry acted to resolve the issue domestically, supported by legislative steps like the [Submission of the Clergy](/wiki/Submission_of_the_Clergy_Act_1533 "Submission of the Clergy Act 1533") (1534) and [Act in Restraint of Appeals](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Appeals_Act_1532 "Ecclesiastical Appeals Act 1532") (1533). The break with Rome reflected a mix of theological conviction, historical precedent, and political necessity, fulfilling a longstanding English desire for ecclesiastical autonomy while addressing immediate dynastic concerns. This laid the foundation for the development of Anglicanism as a distinct national church.

The English Church under Henry VIII continued to maintain Catholic doctrines and liturgical celebrations of the [sacraments](/wiki/Anglican_sacraments "Anglican sacraments") despite its separation from Rome. With little exception, Henry VIII allowed no changes during his lifetime.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussell2010-78) Under [King Edward VI](/wiki/King_Edward_VI "King Edward VI") (1547–1553), however, the church in England first began to undergo what is known as the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), in the course of which it acquired a number of characteristics that would subsequently become recognised as constituting its distinctive "Anglican" identity.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussell201088-79)

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development")]

See also: [History of the Anglican Communion](/wiki/History_of_the_Anglican_Communion "History of the Anglican Communion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Darnley_stage_3.jpg/250px-Darnley_stage_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darnley_stage_3.jpg)

[Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I "Queen Elizabeth I") revived the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") in 1559 and established a uniform faith and practice; she took the title "Supreme Governor"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Frederick_Denison_Maurice._Portrait_c1865.jpg/250px-Frederick_Denison_Maurice._Portrait_c1865.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederick_Denison_Maurice._Portrait_c1865.jpg)

[Frederick Denison Maurice](/wiki/Frederick_Denison_Maurice "Frederick Denison Maurice"), a prominent 19th-century Anglican theologian

With the [Elizabethan Settlement](/wiki/Elizabethan_Settlement "Elizabethan Settlement") of 1559, the Protestant identity of the English and Irish churches was affirmed by means of parliamentary legislation which mandated allegiance and loyalty to the English Crown in all their members. The Elizabethan church began to develop distinct religious traditions, assimilating some of the theology of [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches") with the services in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* (which drew extensively on the [Sarum Rite](/wiki/Sarum_Rite "Sarum Rite") native to England), under the leadership and organisation of a continuing episcopate.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards198389-80) Over the years, these traditions themselves came to command adherence and loyalty. The Elizabethan Settlement stopped the radical Protestant tendencies under Edward VI by combining the more radical elements of the [1552 prayer book](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1552) "Book of Common Prayer (1552)") with the conservative "Catholic" [1549 prayer book](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1549) "Book of Common Prayer (1549)") into the [1559 *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1559) "Book of Common Prayer (1559)"). From then on, Protestantism was in a "state of arrested development", regardless of the attempts to detach the Church of England from its "idiosyncratic anchorage in the medieval past" by various groups which tried to push it towards a more Reformed theology and governance in the years 1560–1660.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch1990171–172-81)

Although two important constitutive elements of what later would emerge as Anglicanism were present in 1559 – scripture, the [historic episcopate](/wiki/Historic_episcopate "Historic episcopate"), the *Book of Common Prayer*, the teachings of the First Four Ecumenical Councils as the yardstick of catholicity, the teaching of the Church Fathers and Catholic bishops, and informed reason – neither the laypeople nor the clergy perceived themselves as Anglicans at the beginning of Elizabeth I's reign, as there was no such identity. Neither does the term *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")* appear until the 1627 to describe a church which refused to identify itself definitely as Catholic or Protestant, or as both, "and had decided in the end that this is virtue rather than a handicap".[[79]](#cite_note-82)

Historical studies on the period 1560–1660 written before the late 1960s tended to project the predominant conformist spirituality and doctrine of the 1660s on the ecclesiastical situation one hundred years before, and there was also a tendency to take polemically binary partitions of reality claimed by contestants studied (such as the dichotomies Protestant-"Popish" or "[Laudian](/wiki/Laudianism "Laudianism")"-"Puritan") at face value. Since the late 1960s, these interpretations have been criticised. Studies on the subject written during the last forty-five years have, however, not reached any consensus on how to interpret this period in English church history. The extent to which one or several positions concerning doctrine and spirituality existed alongside the more well-known and articulate Puritan movement and the Durham House Party, and the exact extent of continental Calvinism among the English elite and among the ordinary churchgoers from the 1560s to the 1620s are subjects of current and ongoing debate.[[d]](#cite_note-83)

In 1662, under [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), a revised *Book of Common Prayer* was produced, which was acceptable to high churchmen as well as some [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") and is still considered authoritative to this day.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack200511,_129-84) In so far as Anglicans derived their identity from both parliamentary legislation and ecclesiastical tradition, a crisis of identity could result wherever secular and religious loyalties came into conflict – and such a crisis indeed occurred in 1776 with the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), most of whose signatories were, at least nominally, Anglican.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards198442-85) For these American patriots, even the forms of Anglican services were in doubt, since the Prayer Book rites of [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins"), [Evensong](/wiki/Evening_Prayer_(Anglican) "Evening Prayer (Anglican)"), and Holy Communion all included specific prayers for the British royal family. Consequently, the conclusion of the [War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") eventually resulted in the creation of two new Anglican churches, the [Episcopal Church in the United States](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") in those states that had achieved independence; and in the 1830s, [the Church of England in Canada](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Anglican Church of Canada") became independent from the Church of England in those North American colonies which had remained under British control and to which many Loyalist churchmen had migrated.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards198443-86)

Reluctantly, legislation was passed in the British Parliament (the Consecration of Bishops Abroad Act 1786) to allow bishops to be consecrated for an American church outside of allegiance to the British Crown (since no dioceses had ever been established in the former American colonies).[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards198443-86) Both in the United States and in Canada, the new Anglican churches developed novel models of self-government, collective decision-making, and self-supported financing; that would be consistent with separation of religious and secular identities.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1984322-87)

In the following century, two further factors acted to accelerate the development of a distinct Anglican identity. From 1828 and 1829, [Dissenters](/wiki/Dissenters "Dissenters") and Catholics could be elected to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"),[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1984113,_124-88) which consequently ceased to be a body drawn purely from the established churches of Scotland, England, and Ireland; but which nevertheless, over the following ten years, engaged in extensive reforming legislation affecting the interests of the English and Irish churches; which, by the [Acts of Union of 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), had been reconstituted as the [United Church of England and Ireland](/wiki/United_Church_of_England_and_Ireland "United Church of England and Ireland") (a union which was dissolved in 1871). The propriety of this legislation was bitterly contested by the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") (Tractarians),[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1984178-89) who in response developed a vision of Anglicanism as religious tradition deriving ultimately from the [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_councils "Ecumenical councils") of the patristic church. Those within the Church of England opposed to the Tractarians, and to their revived ritual practices, introduced a stream of bills in parliament aimed to control innovations in worship.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChadwick1987324-90) This only made the dilemma more acute, with consequent continual litigation in the secular and ecclesiastical courts.

Over the same period, Anglican churches engaged vigorously in [Christian missions](/wiki/Mission_(Christian) "Mission (Christian)"), resulting in the creation, by the end of the century, of over ninety colonial bishoprics,[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1984318-91) which gradually coalesced into new self-governing churches on the Canadian and American models. However, the case of [John Colenso](/wiki/John_Colenso "John Colenso"), [Bishop of Natal](/wiki/Bishop_of_Natal "Bishop of Natal"), reinstated in 1865 by the English [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") over the heads of the Church in South Africa,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1984324-92) demonstrated acutely that the extension of episcopacy had to be accompanied by a recognised Anglican ecclesiology of ecclesiastical authority, distinct from secular power.

Consequently, at the instigation of the bishops of Canada and South Africa, the first [Lambeth Conference](/wiki/Lambeth_Conference "Lambeth Conference") was called in 1867;[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1984325-93) to be followed by further conferences in 1878 and 1888, and thereafter at ten-year intervals. The various papers and declarations of successive Lambeth Conferences have served to frame the continued Anglican debate on identity, especially as relating to the possibility of ecumenical discussion with other churches. This ecumenical aspiration became much more of a possibility, as other denominational groups rapidly followed the example of the Anglican Communion in founding their own transnational alliances: the [Alliance of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Alliance_of_Reformed_Churches "World Alliance of Reformed Churches"), the [Ecumenical Methodist Council](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"), the [International Congregational Council](/wiki/World_Alliance_of_Reformed_Churches "World Alliance of Reformed Churches"), and the [Baptist World Alliance](/wiki/Baptist_World_Alliance "Baptist World Alliance").

### Theories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Theories")]

Leaders of the [Tractarian](/wiki/Tractarian "Tractarian") movement

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Edward_Bouverie_Pusey.jpg/250px-Edward_Bouverie_Pusey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Bouverie_Pusey.jpg)

[Edward Bouverie Pusey](/wiki/Edward_Bouverie_Pusey "Edward Bouverie Pusey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/John-Henry-Newman.gif/250px-John-Henry-Newman.gif)](/wiki/File:John-Henry-Newman.gif)

[John Henry Newman](/wiki/John_Henry_Newman "John Henry Newman")

Anglicanism was seen as a middle way, or *via media*, between two branches of Protestantism, Lutheranism and Reformed Christianity.[[32]](#cite_note-Anglican_and_Episcopal_History-33) In their rejection of absolute parliamentary authority, the [Tractarians](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement"), especially [John Henry Newman](/wiki/John_Henry_Newman "John Henry Newman"), looked back to the writings of 17th-century Anglican divines, finding in these texts the idea of the English church as a *via media* between the Protestant and Catholic traditions.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2003-94) This view was associated – especially in the writings of [Edward Bouverie Pusey](/wiki/Edward_Bouverie_Pusey "Edward Bouverie Pusey") – with the theory of Anglicanism as one of three "[branches](/wiki/Branch_Theory "Branch Theory")" (alongside the Catholic Church and the Orthodox Churches) historically arising out of the common tradition of the earliest [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_councils "Ecumenical councils"). Newman himself subsequently rejected his theory of the *via media*, as essentially historicist and static and hence unable to accommodate any dynamic development within the church.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2003-94) Nevertheless, the aspiration to ground Anglican identity in the writings of the 17th-century divines and in faithfulness to the traditions of the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") reflects a continuing theme of Anglican ecclesiology, most recently in the writings of [Henry Robert McAdoo](/wiki/Henry_Robert_McAdoo "Henry Robert McAdoo").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcAdoo1991-95)

The Tractarian formulation of the theory of the *via media* between Protestantism and Catholicism was essentially a party platform, and not acceptable to Anglicans outside the confines of the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement"). However, this theory of the via media was reworked in the ecclesiological writings of [Frederick Denison Maurice](/wiki/Frederick_Denison_Maurice "Frederick Denison Maurice"), in a more dynamic form that became widely influential. Both Maurice and Newman saw the Church of England of their day as sorely deficient in faith; but whereas Newman had looked back to a distant past when the light of faith might have appeared to burn brighter, Maurice looked forward to the possibility of a brighter revelation of faith in the future. Maurice saw the Protestant and Catholic strands within the Church of England as contrary but complementary, both maintaining elements of the true church, but incomplete without the other; such that a true catholic and evangelical church might come into being by a union of opposites.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESykes197816-96)

Central to Maurice's perspective was his belief that the collective elements of family, nation, and church represented a divine order of structures through which God unfolds his continuing work of creation. Hence, for Maurice, the Protestant tradition had maintained the elements of national distinction which were amongst the marks of the true universal church, but which had been lost within contemporary Catholicism in the internationalism of centralised papal authority. Within the coming universal church that Maurice foresaw, national churches would each maintain the six signs of catholicity: baptism, Eucharist, the creeds, Scripture, an episcopal ministry, and a fixed liturgy (which could take a variety of forms in accordance with divinely ordained distinctions in national characteristics).[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2003-94) This vision of a becoming universal church as a congregation of autonomous national churches proved highly congenial in Anglican circles; and Maurice's six signs were adapted to form the [Chicago-Lambeth Quadrilateral](/wiki/Chicago-Lambeth_Quadrilateral "Chicago-Lambeth Quadrilateral") of 1888.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodhouse-Hawkins1988-97)

In the latter decades of the 20th century, Maurice's theory, and the various strands of Anglican thought that derived from it, have been criticised by [Stephen Sykes](/wiki/Stephen_Sykes "Stephen Sykes"),[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESykes197819-98) who argues that the terms *Protestant* and *Catholic* as used in these approaches are synthetic constructs denoting ecclesiastic identities unacceptable to those to whom the labels are applied. Hence, the Catholic Church does not regard itself as a party or strand within the universal church – but rather identifies itself as the universal church. Moreover, Sykes criticises the proposition, implicit in theories of *via media*, that there is no distinctive body of Anglican doctrines, other than those of the universal church; accusing this of being an excuse not to undertake systematic doctrine at all.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESykes197853-99)

Contrariwise, Sykes notes a high degree of commonality in Anglican liturgical forms and in the doctrinal understandings expressed within those liturgies. He proposes that Anglican identity might rather be found within a shared consistent pattern of prescriptive liturgies, established and maintained through [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law_of_the_Anglican_Communion "Canon law of the Anglican Communion"), and embodying both a historic deposit of formal statements of doctrine, and also framing the regular reading and proclamation of scripture.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESykes197844-100) Sykes nevertheless agrees with those heirs of Maurice who emphasise the incompleteness of Anglicanism as a positive feature, and quotes with qualified approval the words of [Michael Ramsey](/wiki/Michael_Ramsey "Michael Ramsey"):

> For while the Anglican church is vindicated by its place in history, with a strikingly balanced witness to Gospel and Church and sound learning, its greater vindication lies in its pointing through its own history to something of which it is a fragment. Its credentials are its incompleteness, with the tension and the travail of its soul. It is clumsy and untidy, it baffles neatness and logic. For it is not sent to commend itself as 'the best type of Christianity,' but by its very brokenness to point to the universal Church wherein all have died.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsey1936220-101)

## Doctrine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Doctrine")]

Main article: [Anglican doctrine](/wiki/Anglican_doctrine "Anglican doctrine")

### "Catholic and reformed"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: \"Catholic and reformed\"")]

The distinction between Reformed and Catholic, and the coherence of the two, is a matter of debate within the Anglican Communion. The [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") of the mid-19th century revived and extended doctrinal, liturgical, and pastoral practices similar to those of Roman Catholicism. This extends beyond the ceremony of [high church](/wiki/High_church "High church") services to even more theologically significant territory, such as sacramental theology (see [Anglican sacraments](/wiki/Anglican_sacraments "Anglican sacraments")). While [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") practices, particularly liturgical ones, have become more common within the tradition over the last century, there are also places where practices and beliefs resonate more closely with the evangelical movements of the 1730s (see [Sydney Anglicanism](/wiki/Sydney_Anglicanism "Sydney Anglicanism")).

### Guiding principles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Guiding principles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Hooker-Statue.jpeg/250px-Hooker-Statue.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Hooker-Statue.jpeg)

[Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker_(theologian) "Richard Hooker (theologian)") (1554–1600), one of the most influential figures in shaping Anglican theology and self-identity

For high-church Anglicans, doctrine is neither established by a [magisterium](/wiki/Magisterium "Magisterium"), nor derived from the theology of an [eponymous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/eponym "wikt:eponym") founder (such as [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism")), nor summed up in a confession of faith beyond the ecumenical [creeds](/wiki/Creed "Creed"), such as the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") [Book of Concord](/wiki/Book_of_Concord "Book of Concord"). For them, the earliest Anglican theological documents are its prayer books, which they see as the products of profound theological reflection, compromise, and synthesis. They emphasise the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* as a key expression of Anglican doctrine. The principle of looking to the prayer books as a guide to the parameters of belief and practice is called by the Latin name *[lex orandi, lex credendi](/wiki/Lex_orandi,_lex_credendi "Lex orandi, lex credendi")* ("the law of prayer is the law of belief").

Within the prayer books are the fundamentals of Anglican doctrine: the [Apostles'](/wiki/Apostles%27_Creed "Apostles' Creed") and [Nicene](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") creeds, the [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed") (now rarely used), the scriptures (via the lectionary), the sacraments, daily prayer, the [catechism](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism"), and apostolic succession in the context of the historic threefold ministry. For some low-church and evangelical Anglicans, the 16th-century [Reformed](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") Thirty-Nine Articles form the basis of doctrine.

#### Distinctives of Anglican belief

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Distinctives of Anglican belief")]

The [Thirty-Nine Articles](/wiki/Thirty-Nine_Articles "Thirty-Nine Articles") played a significant role in Anglican doctrine and practice. Following the passing of the 1604 canons, all Anglican clergy had to formally subscribe to the articles. Today, however, the articles are no longer binding,[[98]](#cite_note-102) but are seen as a historical document which has played a significant role in the shaping of Anglican identity. The degree to which each of the articles has remained influential varies.

On the doctrine of [justification](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)"), for example, there is a wide range of beliefs within the Anglican Communion, with some [Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") arguing for a faith with good works and the sacraments. At the same time, however, some [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") Anglicans ascribe to the Reformed emphasis on *[sola fide](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide")* ("faith alone") in their doctrine of justification (see [Sydney Anglicanism](/wiki/Sydney_Anglicanism "Sydney Anglicanism")). Still other Anglicans adopt a nuanced view of justification, taking elements from the early [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"), [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), [liberal theology](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity"), and [latitudinarian](/wiki/Latitudinarian "Latitudinarian") thought.

Arguably, the most influential of the original articles has been Article VI on the "sufficiency of scripture", which says that "Scripture containeth all things necessary to salvation: so that whatsoever is not read therein, nor may be proved thereby, is not to be required of any man, that it should be believed as an article of the Faith, or be thought requisite or necessary to salvation." This article has informed Anglican biblical [exegesis](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis") and [hermeneutics](/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics") since earliest times.

Anglicans look for authority in their "standard divines" (see below). Historically, the most influential of these – apart from Cranmer – has been the 16th-century cleric and theologian [Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker_(theologian) "Richard Hooker (theologian)"), who after 1660 was increasingly portrayed as the founding father of Anglicanism. Hooker's description of Anglican authority as being derived primarily from scripture, informed by reason (the intellect and the experience of God) and tradition (the practices and beliefs of the historical church), has influenced Anglican self-identity and doctrinal reflection perhaps more powerfully than any other formula. The analogy of the "three-legged stool" of [scripture](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), [reason](/wiki/Reason "Reason"), and [tradition](/wiki/Sacred_Tradition "Sacred Tradition") is often incorrectly attributed to Hooker. Rather, Hooker's description is a hierarchy of authority, with scripture as foundational and reason and tradition as vitally important, but secondary, authorities.

Finally, the extension of Anglicanism into non-English cultures, the growing diversity of prayer books, and the increasing interest in ecumenical dialogue have led to further reflection on the parameters of Anglican identity. Many Anglicans look to the [Chicago-Lambeth Quadrilateral](/wiki/Chicago-Lambeth_Quadrilateral "Chicago-Lambeth Quadrilateral") of 1888 as the *sine qua non* of communal identity.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESydnor198080-103) In brief, the quadrilateral's four points are the scriptures as containing all things necessary to salvation; the creeds (specifically, the Apostles' and Nicene Creeds) as the sufficient statement of Christian faith; the dominical sacraments of [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") and [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion"); and the historic [episcopate](/wiki/Episcopalian_church_governance#Anglican_Communion "Episcopalian church governance").[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESydnor198080-103)

### Divines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Divines")]

See also: [Richard Neile](/wiki/Richard_Neile "Richard Neile"), [Caroline Divines](/wiki/Caroline_Divines "Caroline Divines"), [Hackney Phalanx](/wiki/Hackney_Phalanx "Hackney Phalanx"), and [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg/250px-Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg)

[Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), author of the first two editions of the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*

Within the Anglican tradition, "divines" are clergy of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") whose theological writings have been considered standards for faith, doctrine, worship, and spirituality, and whose influence has permeated the Anglican Communion in varying degrees through the years.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooty1998175–176,_197-104) While there is no authoritative list of these Anglican divines, there are some whose names would likely be found on most lists – those who are commemorated in [lesser feasts](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints_(Anglican) "Calendar of saints (Anglican)") of the Anglican churches and those whose works are frequently [anthologised](/wiki/Anthology "Anthology").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooty1998163,_174-105)

The corpus produced by Anglican divines is diverse. What they have in common is a commitment to the faith as conveyed by scripture and the *Book of Common Prayer*, thus regarding prayer and theology in a manner akin to that of the [Apostolic Fathers](/wiki/Apostolic_Fathers "Apostolic Fathers").[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooty1998163-106) On the whole, Anglican divines view the *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")* of Anglicanism not as a compromise, but as "a positive position, witnessing to the universality of God and God's kingdom working through the fallible, earthly *ecclesia Anglicana*".[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooty1998164-107)

These theologians regard scripture as interpreted through tradition and reason as authoritative in matters concerning salvation. Reason and tradition, indeed, are extant in and presupposed by scripture, thus implying co-operation between God and humanity, God and nature, and between the sacred and secular. Faith is thus regarded as [incarnational](/wiki/Incarnation "Incarnation") and authority as dispersed.

Amongst the early Anglican divines of the 16th and 17th centuries, the names of [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), [John Jewel](/wiki/John_Jewel "John Jewel"), [Matthew Parker](/wiki/Matthew_Parker "Matthew Parker"), [Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker "Richard Hooker"), [Lancelot Andrewes](/wiki/Lancelot_Andrewes "Lancelot Andrewes"), and [Jeremy Taylor](/wiki/Jeremy_Taylor "Jeremy Taylor") predominate. The influential character of Hooker's *[Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity](/wiki/Of_the_Laws_of_Ecclesiastical_Polity "Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity")* cannot be overestimated. Published in 1593 and subsequently, Hooker's eight-volume work is primarily a treatise on church-state relations, but it deals comprehensively with issues of [biblical interpretation](/wiki/Biblical_interpretation "Biblical interpretation"), [soteriology](/wiki/Soteriology "Soteriology"), ethics, and [sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity"). Throughout the work, Hooker makes clear that theology involves prayer and is concerned with ultimate issues and that theology is relevant to the social mission of the church.

The 17th century saw the rise of two important movements in Anglicanism: [Cambridge Platonism](/wiki/Cambridge_Platonists "Cambridge Platonists"), with its mystical understanding of reason as the "candle of the Lord", and the [Evangelical Revival](/wiki/Evangelical_Revival "Evangelical Revival"), with its emphasis on the personal experience of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"). The Cambridge Platonist movement evolved into a school called [Latitudinarianism](/wiki/Latitudinarianism "Latitudinarianism"), which emphasised reason as the barometer of discernment and took a stance of indifference towards doctrinal and ecclesiological differences.

The Evangelical Revival, influenced by such figures as [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley") and [Charles Simeon](/wiki/Charles_Simeon "Charles Simeon"), re-emphasised the importance of [justification through faith](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide") and the consequent importance of personal conversion. Some in this movement, such as Wesley and [George Whitefield](/wiki/George_Whitefield "George Whitefield"), took the message to the United States, influencing the [First Great Awakening](/wiki/First_Great_Awakening "First Great Awakening") and creating an Anglo-American movement called [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") that would eventually break away, structurally, from the Anglican churches after the American Revolution.

By the 19th century, there was a renewed interest in pre-Reformation English religious thought and practice. Theologians such as [John Keble](/wiki/John_Keble "John Keble"), [Edward Bouverie Pusey](/wiki/Edward_Bouverie_Pusey "Edward Bouverie Pusey"), and [John Henry Newman](/wiki/John_Henry_Newman "John Henry Newman") had widespread influence in the realm of polemics, homiletics and theological and devotional works, not least because they largely repudiated the old high-church tradition and replaced it with a dynamic appeal to antiquity which looked beyond the Reformers and Anglican formularies.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENockles19947–8,_113,_125,_127-108) Their work is largely credited with the development of the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement"), which sought to reassert Catholic identity and practice in Anglicanism.[[105]](#cite_note-109)

In contrast to this movement, clergy such as the Bishop of Liverpool, [J. C. Ryle](/wiki/J._C._Ryle "J. C. Ryle"), sought to uphold the distinctly Reformed identity of the Church of England. He was not a servant of the status quo, but argued for a lively religion which emphasised grace, holy and charitable living, and the plain use of the 1662 *Book of Common Prayer* (interpreted in a partisan evangelical way)[[e]](#cite_note-110) without additional rituals. [Frederick Denison Maurice](/wiki/Frederick_Denison_Maurice "Frederick Denison Maurice"), through such works as *The Kingdom of Christ*, played a pivotal role in inaugurating another movement, [Christian socialism](/wiki/Christian_socialism "Christian socialism"). In this, Maurice transformed Hooker's emphasis on the [incarnational](/wiki/Incarnation "Incarnation") nature of Anglican spirituality to an imperative for social justice.

In the 19th century, Anglican biblical scholarship began to assume a distinct character, represented by the so-called "Cambridge triumvirate" of [Joseph Lightfoot](/wiki/Joseph_Lightfoot "Joseph Lightfoot"), [F. J. A. Hort](/wiki/Fenton_John_Anthony_Hort "Fenton John Anthony Hort"), and [Brooke Foss Westcott](/wiki/Brooke_Foss_Westcott "Brooke Foss Westcott").[[106]](#cite_note-111) Their orientation is best summed up by Westcott's observation that "Life which Christ is and which Christ communicates, the life which fills our whole beings as we realise its capacities, is active fellowship with God."[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooty1998183-112)[[108]](#cite_note-113)

The earlier part of the 20th century is marked by [Charles Gore](/wiki/Charles_Gore "Charles Gore"), with his emphasis on [natural revelation](/wiki/Natural_revelation "Natural revelation"), and [William Temple](/wiki/William_Temple_(bishop) "William Temple (bishop)")'s focus on Christianity and society, while, from outside England, [Robert Leighton](/wiki/Robert_Leighton_(bishop) "Robert Leighton (bishop)"), Archbishop of Glasgow, and several clergy from the United States have been suggested, such as [William Porcher DuBose](/wiki/William_Porcher_DuBose "William Porcher DuBose"), [John Henry Hobart](/wiki/John_Henry_Hobart "John Henry Hobart") (1775–1830, Bishop of New York 1816–30), [William Meade](/wiki/William_Meade "William Meade"), [Phillips Brooks](/wiki/Phillips_Brooks "Phillips Brooks"), and [Charles Brent](/wiki/Charles_Brent "Charles Brent").[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooty1998164,_171–172-114)

### Churchmanship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Churchmanship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c6/Tridentine_mass.jpg/250px-Tridentine_mass.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tridentine_mass.jpg)

An eastward-facing [Solemn High Mass](/wiki/Solemn_High_Mass "Solemn High Mass"), a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") liturgical phenomenon which reemerged in Anglicanism following the [Catholic Revival](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") of the 19th century

*[Churchmanship](/wiki/Churchmanship "Churchmanship")* can be defined as the manifestation of theology in the realms of liturgy, piety and, to some extent, spirituality. Anglican diversity in this respect has tended to reflect the diversity in the tradition's Reformed and Catholic identity. Different individuals, groups, parishes, dioceses and provinces may identify more closely with one or the other, or some mixture of the two.

The range of Anglican belief and practice became particularly divisive during the 19th century, when some clergy were disciplined and even imprisoned on charges of introducing illegal ritual while, at the same time, others were criticised for engaging in public worship services with ministers of Reformed churches. Resistance to the growing acceptance and restoration of traditional Catholic ceremonial by the mainstream of Anglicanism ultimately led to the formation of small breakaway churches such as the [Free Church of England](/wiki/Free_Church_of_England "Free Church of England") in England (1844) and the [Reformed Episcopal Church](/wiki/Reformed_Episcopal_Church "Reformed Episcopal Church") in North America (1873).[[110]](#cite_note-115)[[111]](#cite_note-116)

[Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") (and some broad-church) Anglicans celebrate public liturgy in ways that understand worship to be something very special and of utmost importance. [Vestments](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments") are worn by the clergy, sung settings are often used, and [incense](/wiki/Incense "Incense") may be used. Nowadays, in most Anglican churches, the Eucharist is celebrated in a manner similar to the usage of [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics") and some [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), though, in many churches, more traditional, "pre–Vatican II" models of worship are common (e.g., an "eastward orientation" at the altar). Whilst many Anglo-Catholics derive much of their liturgical practice from that of the pre-Reformation English church, others more closely follow traditional Roman Catholic practices.

The Eucharist may sometimes be celebrated in the form known as [High Mass](/wiki/Solemn_Mass "Solemn Mass"), with a priest, deacon and [subdeacon](/wiki/Subdeacon "Subdeacon") (usually actually a layman) dressed in traditional vestments, with incense and [sanctus bells](/wiki/Altar_bell "Altar bell") and prayers adapted from the [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal") or other sources by the celebrant. Such churches may also have forms of [eucharistic adoration](/wiki/Eucharistic_adoration "Eucharistic adoration") such as [Benediction of the Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Benediction_of_the_Blessed_Sacrament "Benediction of the Blessed Sacrament"). In terms of personal piety, some Anglicans may recite the [Rosary](/wiki/Anglican_Rosary "Anglican Rosary") and [Angelus](/wiki/Angelus "Angelus"), be involved in a devotional society dedicated to "Our Lady" (the [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Blessed Virgin Mary")) and seek the intercession of the saints.

In recent decades, the prayer books of several provinces have, out of deference to a greater agreement with Eastern [Conciliarism](/wiki/Conciliarism "Conciliarism") (and a perceived greater respect accorded Anglicanism by Eastern Orthodoxy than by Roman Catholicism), instituted a number of historically Eastern and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox") elements in their liturgies, including introduction of the [Trisagion](/wiki/Trisagion "Trisagion") and deletion of the [filioque](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque") clause from the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed").

For their part, those [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") (and some broad-church) Anglicans who emphasise the more Protestant aspects of the Church stress the Reformation theme of [salvation](/wiki/Salvation#Christian_views_of_salvation "Salvation") by grace through faith. They emphasise the two dominical sacraments of Baptism and Eucharist, viewing the other five as "lesser rites". Some evangelical Anglicans may even tend to take the inerrancy of scripture literally, adopting the view of Article VI that it contains all things necessary to salvation in an explicit sense. Worship in churches influenced by these principles tends to be significantly less elaborate, with greater emphasis on the [Liturgy of the Word](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Word "Liturgy of the Word") (the reading of the scriptures, the sermon, and the intercessory prayers).

The Order for Holy Communion may be celebrated bi-weekly or monthly (in preference to the [daily offices](/wiki/Daily_office "Daily office")), by priests attired in [choir habit](/wiki/Choir_habit "Choir habit"), or more regular clothes, rather than Eucharistic vestments. Ceremony may be in keeping with their view of the provisions of the 17th-century Puritans – being a [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") interpretation of the [Ornaments Rubric](/wiki/Ornaments_Rubric "Ornaments Rubric") – no candles, no incense, no bells, and a minimum of manual actions by the presiding celebrant (such as touching the elements at the [Words of Institution](/wiki/Words_of_Institution "Words of Institution")).

In the early 21st century, there has been a growth of [charismatic](/wiki/Charismatic_Movement "Charismatic Movement") worship among Anglicans. Both Anglo-Catholics and evangelicals have been affected by this movement such that it is not uncommon to find typically charismatic postures, music, and other themes evident during the services of otherwise Anglo-Catholic or evangelical parishes.

The spectrum of Anglican beliefs and practice is too large to be fit into these labels. Many Anglicans locate themselves somewhere in the spectrum of the broad-church tradition and consider themselves an amalgam of evangelical and Catholic. Such Anglicans stress that Anglicanism is the *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")* (middle way) between the two major strains of Western Christianity and that Anglicanism is like a "bridge" between the two strains.

### Sacramental doctrine and practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sacramental doctrine and practice")]

Main article: [Anglican sacraments](/wiki/Anglican_sacraments "Anglican sacraments")

In accord with its prevailing self-identity as a *[via media](/wiki/Via_media "Via media")* or "middle path" of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), Anglican sacramental theology expresses elements in keeping with its status as being both a church in the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicity "Catholicity") tradition as well as a [Reformed](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") church. With respect to sacramental theology, the Catholic heritage is perhaps most strongly asserted in the importance Anglicanism places on the [sacraments](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") as a means of [grace](/wiki/Grace_in_Christianity "Grace in Christianity"), [sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity"), and [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity"), as expressed in the church's [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") and [doctrine](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine").

Of the seven sacraments, all Anglicans recognise [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") and the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") as being directly instituted by Christ. The other five – [Confession/Absolution](/wiki/Confession_(sacrament) "Confession (sacrament)"), [Matrimony](/wiki/Christian_view_of_marriage "Christian view of marriage"), [Confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation_(sacrament) "Confirmation (sacrament)"), [Holy Orders](/wiki/Holy_Orders "Holy Orders") (also called Ordination), and [Anointing of the Sick](/wiki/Anointing_of_the_Sick "Anointing of the Sick") (also called Unction) – are regarded variously as full sacraments by [Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") and many [high church](/wiki/High_church "High church") and some [broad-church](/wiki/Broad_church "Broad church") Anglicans, but merely as "sacramental rites" by other broad-church and [low-church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") Anglicans, especially [evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") associated with [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_(Anglican) "Reform (Anglican)") and the [Diocese of Sydney](/wiki/Anglican_Diocese_of_Sydney "Anglican Diocese of Sydney").

#### Eucharistic theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Eucharistic theology")]

Main article: [Anglican eucharistic theology](/wiki/Anglican_eucharistic_theology "Anglican eucharistic theology")

Anglican eucharistic theology is divergent in practice, reflecting the essential comprehensiveness of the tradition. A few [low-church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") Anglicans take a strictly memorialist ([Zwinglian](/wiki/Zwingli "Zwingli")) view of the sacrament. In other words, they see Holy Communion as a memorial to Christ's suffering, and participation in the Eucharist as both a re-enactment of the Last Supper and a foreshadowing of the heavenly banquet – the fulfilment of the eucharistic promise.

Other low-church Anglicans believe in the [real presence of Christ in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist") but deny that the presence of Christ is carnal or is necessarily localised in the bread and wine. Despite explicit criticism in the [Thirty-Nine Articles](/wiki/Thirty-Nine_Articles "Thirty-Nine Articles"), many high-church or Anglo-Catholic Anglicans hold, more or less, the Catholic view of the real presence as expressed in the doctrine of [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation"), seeing the Eucharist as a liturgical representation of Christ's atoning sacrifice with the elements actually transformed into Christ's body and blood.

The majority of Anglicans, however, have in common a belief in the real presence, defined in one way or another. To that extent, they are in the company of the continental reformer [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") and Calvin rather than [Ulrich Zwingli](/wiki/Ulrich_Zwingli "Ulrich Zwingli"). The Catechism of the American BCP of 1976 repeats the standard Anglican view ("The outward and visible sign in the Eucharist is the bread and wine"..."The inward and spiritual grace in the Holy Communion is the Body and Blood of Christ given to his people, and received by faith") without further definition. It should be remembered that Anglicanism has no official doctrine on this matter, believing it is wiser to leave the Presence a mystery. The faithful can believe privately whatever explanation they favour, be it transubstantiation, consubstantiation, receptionism, or virtualism (the two[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] most congenial to Anglicans for centuries until the Oxford Movement), each of which espouses belief in the real presence in one way or another, or memorialism, which has never been an option with Anglicans.

A famous Anglican aphorism regarding Christ's presence in the sacrament, commonly misattributed to [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), is first found in print in a poem by [John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"):[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMay200754–55-117)

> He was the word that spake it,  
> He took the bread and brake it:  
> And what that word did make it,  
> I do believe and take it.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMay200755,_66-118)

An Anglican position on the eucharistic sacrifice ("Sacrifice of the Mass") was expressed in the response *[Saepius officio](/wiki/Saepius_officio "Saepius officio")* of the archbishops of Canterbury and York to [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII")'s papal encyclical *[Apostolicae curae](/wiki/Apostolicae_curae "Apostolicae curae")*: viz. that the Prayer Book contained a strong sacrificial theology. Later revisions of the Prayer Book influenced by the Scottish Canon of 1764 first adopted by the Protestant Episcopal Church in 1789 made this assertion quite evident: "we do make and celebrate before thy Divine Majesty with these thy holy gifts, which we now *offer* unto thee, the memorial thy Son has commanded us to make", which is repeated in the 1929 English BCP and included in such words or others such as "present" or "show forth" in subsequent revisions.

Anglican and Roman Catholic representatives declared that they had "substantial agreement on the doctrine of the Eucharist" in the *Windsor Statement on Eucharistic Doctrine* by the Anglican-Roman Catholic International Consultation (1971)[[114]](#cite_note-119) and the Elucidation of the ARCIC Windsor Statement (1979). The final response (1991) to these documents by the Vatican made it plain that it did not consider the degree of agreement reached to be satisfactory.

## Practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Practices")]

Further information on the daily Anglican morning office: [Morning Prayer (Anglican)](/wiki/Morning_Prayer_(Anglican) "Morning Prayer (Anglican)")

See also: [Evening Prayer (Anglican)](/wiki/Evening_Prayer_(Anglican) "Evening Prayer (Anglican)") and [Prayer of Humble Access](/wiki/Prayer_of_Humble_Access "Prayer of Humble Access")

In Anglicanism there is a distinction between liturgy, which is the formal public and communal worship of the church, and personal prayer and devotion, which may be public or private. Liturgy is regulated by the prayer books and consists of the Eucharist (some call it Holy Communion or Mass), the other six sacraments, and the daily offices such as Morning Prayer and Evening Prayer.

### *Book of Common Prayer*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Book of Common Prayer")]

Main article: [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Book_of_common_prayer_1596.jpg/250px-Book_of_common_prayer_1596.jpg)](/wiki/File:Book_of_common_prayer_1596.jpg)

The 1596 *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*

The *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* (BCP) is the foundational prayer book of Anglicanism. The original book of 1549 (revised in 1552) was one of the instruments of the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), replacing the various "uses" or rites in Latin that had been used in different parts of the country with a single compact volume in the language of the people, so that "now from henceforth all the Realm shall have but one use". Suppressed under Queen [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England"), it was revised in 1559, and then again in 1662, after the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"). This version was made mandatory in England and Wales by the [Act of Uniformity](/wiki/Act_of_Uniformity_1662 "Act of Uniformity 1662") and was in standard use until the mid-20th century.

With [British colonial](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") expansion from the 17th century onwards, Anglican churches were planted around the globe. These churches at first used and then revised the *Book of Common Prayer* until they, like their parent church, produced prayer books which took into account the developments in liturgical study and practice in the 19th and 20th centuries, which come under the general heading of the [Liturgical Movement](/wiki/Liturgical_Movement "Liturgical Movement").

### Worship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Worship")]

See also: [Church of England parish church](/wiki/Church_of_England_parish_church "Church of England parish church")

Anglican worship services are open to all visitors. Anglican worship originates principally in the reforms of [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), who aimed to create a set order of service like that of the pre-Reformation church but less complex in its seasonal variety and said in English rather than [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). This use of a set order of service is not unlike the Catholic tradition. Traditionally, the pattern was that laid out in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*. Although many Anglican churches now use a wide range of modern service books written in the local language, the structures of the *Book of Common Prayer* are largely retained. Churches which call themselves Anglican will have identified themselves so because they use some form or variant of the *Book of Common Prayer* in the shaping of their worship.

Anglican worship, however, is as diverse as Anglican theology. A contemporary "[low church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church")" service may differ little from the worship of many mainstream non-Anglican Protestant churches. The service is constructed around a sermon focused on Biblical exposition and opened with one or more Bible readings and closed by a series of prayers (both set and extemporised) and hymns or songs. A "[high church](/wiki/High_church "High church")" or Anglo-Catholic service, by contrast, is usually a more formal [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") celebrated by clergy in distinctive [vestments](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments") and may be almost indistinguishable from a Roman Catholic service, often resembling the "pre–Vatican II" [Tridentine rite](/wiki/Tridentine_Mass "Tridentine Mass").

Between these extremes are a variety of styles of worship, often involving a robed choir and the use of the organ to accompany the singing and to provide music before and after the service.
