Anglican churches tend to have [pews](/wiki/Pew "Pew") or chairs, and it is usual for the congregation to kneel for some prayers but to stand for hymns and other parts of the service such as the Gloria, Collect, Gospel reading, Creed and either the Preface or all of the Eucharistic Prayer. Anglicans may genuflect or cross themselves in the same way as Roman Catholics.

Other more traditional Anglicans tend to follow the 1662 *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* and retain the use of the King James Bible. This is typical in many Anglican cathedrals and particularly in [royal peculiars](/wiki/Royal_peculiar "Royal peculiar") such as the [Savoy Chapel](/wiki/Savoy_Chapel "Savoy Chapel") and the [Queen's Chapel](/wiki/Queen%27s_Chapel "Queen's Chapel"). These Anglican church services include classical music instead of songs, hymns from the [New English Hymnal](/wiki/New_English_Hymnal "New English Hymnal") (usually excluding modern hymns such as "Lord of the Dance"), and are generally non-evangelical and formal in practice. Until the mid-20th century the main Sunday service was typically [Morning Prayer](/wiki/Morning_Prayer_(Anglican) "Morning Prayer (Anglican)"), but the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") has once again become the standard form of Sunday worship in most Anglican churches; this again is similar to Roman Catholic practice. Other common Sunday services include an early morning Eucharist without music, an abbreviated Eucharist following a service of morning prayer, and a service of [Evening Prayer](/wiki/Evening_Prayer_(Anglican) "Evening Prayer (Anglican)"), often called "Evensong" when sung, usually celebrated between 3:00 and 6:00 pm. The late-evening service of [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline") was revived in parish use in the early 20th century. Many Anglican churches will also have daily morning and evening prayer, and some have midweek or even daily celebration of the Eucharist.

An Anglican service (whether or not a Eucharist) will include readings from the Bible that are generally taken from a standardised [lectionary](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary"), which provides for much of the Bible (and some passages from the [Apocrypha](/wiki/Biblical_apocrypha "Biblical apocrypha")) to be read out loud in the church over a cycle of one, two, or three years (depending on which eucharistic and office lectionaries are used, respectively). The [sermon](/wiki/Sermon "Sermon") (or [homily](/wiki/Homily "Homily")) is typically about ten to twenty minutes in length, often comparably short to sermons in evangelical churches. Even in the most informal Anglican services, it is common for set prayers such as the weekly [Collect](/wiki/Collect "Collect") to be read. There are also set forms for [intercessory prayer](/wiki/Intercessory_prayer "Intercessory prayer"), though this is now more often extemporaneous. In high and Anglo-Catholic churches there are generally prayers for the dead. Although Anglican public worship is usually ordered according to the canonically approved services, in practice many Anglican churches use forms of service outside these norms. Liberal churches may use freely structured or experimental forms of worship, including patterns borrowed from ecumenical traditions such as those of the [Taizé Community](/wiki/Taiz%C3%A9_Community "Taizé Community") or the [Iona Community](/wiki/Iona_Community "Iona Community").

[Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") parishes might use the modern Roman Catholic liturgy of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") or more traditional forms, such as the [Tridentine Mass](/wiki/Tridentine_Mass "Tridentine Mass") (which is translated into English in the [English Missal](/wiki/English_Missal "English Missal")), the [Anglican Missal](/wiki/Anglican_Missal "Anglican Missal"), or, less commonly, the [Sarum Rite](/wiki/Sarum_Rite "Sarum Rite"). Catholic devotions such as the [Rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary"), [Angelus](/wiki/Angelus "Angelus"), and [Benediction of the Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Benediction_of_the_Blessed_Sacrament "Benediction of the Blessed Sacrament") are also common among Anglo-Catholics.

#### Eucharistic discipline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Eucharistic discipline")]

Only [baptised](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") persons are eligible to receive communion,[[115]](#cite_note-communion-120) although in many churches communion is restricted to those who have not only been baptised but also [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation"). In many Anglican provinces, however, all baptised Christians are now often invited to receive communion and some dioceses have regularised a system for admitting baptised young people to communion before they are confirmed.

The discipline of fasting before communion is practised by some Anglicans. Most Anglican priests require the presence of at least one other person for the celebration of the Eucharist (referring back to Christ's statement in Matthew 18:20, "When two or more are gathered in my name, I will be in the midst of them."), though some Anglo-Catholic priests (like Roman Catholic priests) may say private Masses. As in the Roman Catholic Church, it is a canonical requirement to use fermented wine for communion.

Unlike in Roman Catholicism, the consecrated bread and wine are normally offered to the congregation at a eucharistic service ("communion in both kinds"). This practice is becoming more frequent in the Roman Catholic Church as well, especially through the [Neocatechumenal Way](/wiki/Neocatechumenal_Way "Neocatechumenal Way"). In some churches, the sacrament is reserved in a tabernacle or [aumbry](/wiki/Aumbry "Aumbry") with a lighted candle or lamp nearby. In Anglican churches, only a priest or a bishop may be the celebrant at the Eucharist.

### Daily office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Daily office")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Evensong_in_York_Minster.jpg/250px-Evensong_in_York_Minster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evensong_in_York_Minster.jpg)

[Evensong](/wiki/Evensong "Evensong") at [York Minster](/wiki/York_Minster "York Minster") in [York](/wiki/York "York"), England

Main article: [Daily Office (Anglican)](/wiki/Daily_Office_(Anglican) "Daily Office (Anglican)")

All Anglican prayer books contain offices for Morning Prayer and Evening Prayer (called [Evensong](/wiki/Evensong "Evensong") when sung chorally). In the original *Book of Common Prayer*, these were derived from combinations of the ancient monastic offices of [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") and [Lauds](/wiki/Lauds "Lauds"); and [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") and [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline"), respectively. The prayer offices have an important place in Anglican history.

Prior to the [Catholic revival](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") of the 19th century, which eventually restored the Eucharist as the principal Sunday liturgy, and especially during the 18th century, a morning service combining Matins, the [Litany](/wiki/Litany "Litany"), and ante-Communion comprised the usual expression of common worship, while Matins and Evensong were sung daily in cathedrals and some collegiate chapels. This nurtured a tradition of distinctive [Anglican chant](/wiki/Anglican_chant "Anglican chant") applied to the [canticles](/wiki/Canticle "Canticle") and [psalms](/wiki/Psalm "Psalm") used at the offices (although [plainsong](/wiki/Plainsong "Plainsong") is often used as well).

In some official and many unofficial Anglican service books, these offices are supplemented by other offices such as the [Little Hours](/wiki/Little_Hours "Little Hours") of [Prime](/wiki/Prime_(liturgy) "Prime (liturgy)") and prayer during the day such as ([Terce](/wiki/Terce "Terce"), [Sext](/wiki/Sext "Sext"), [None](/wiki/None_(liturgy) "None (liturgy)"), and [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline")). Some Anglican monastic communities have a [Daily Office](/wiki/Daily_Office "Daily Office") based on that of the *Book of Common Prayer* but with additional antiphons and canticles, etc., for specific days of the week, specific psalms, etc. See, for example, [Order of the Holy Cross](/wiki/Order_of_the_Holy_Cross "Order of the Holy Cross")[[116]](#cite_note-121) and Order of St Helena, editors, *A Monastic Breviary* (Wilton, Conn.: Morehouse-Barlow, 1976). The All Saints Sisters of the Poor,[[117]](#cite_note-122) with convents in Catonsville, Maryland, and elsewhere, use an elaborated version of the Anglican Daily Office. The [Society of St. Francis](/wiki/Society_of_St._Francis "Society of St. Francis") publishes *Celebrating Common Prayer*, which has become especially popular for use among Anglicans.

In England, the United States, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and some other Anglican provinces, the modern prayer books contain four offices:

* Morning Prayer, corresponding to Matins, Lauds and Prime;
* Prayer During the Day, roughly corresponding to the combination of Terce, Sext, and None (Noonday Prayer in the USA);
* Evening Prayer, corresponding to Vespers (and Compline);
* Compline.

In addition, most prayer books include a section of prayers and devotions for family use. In the US, these offices are further supplemented by an "Order of Worship for the Evening", a prelude to or an abbreviated form of Evensong, partly derived from [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") prayers. In the United Kingdom, the publication of *Daily Prayer*, the third volume of *[Common Worship](/wiki/Common_Worship "Common Worship")*, was published in 2005. It retains the services for Morning and Evening Prayer and Compline and includes a section entitled "Prayer during the Day". *A New Zealand Prayer Book* of 1989 provides different outlines for Matins and Evensong on each day of the week, as well as "Midday Prayer", "Night Prayer" and "Family Prayer".

Some Anglicans who pray the office on daily basis use the present [Divine Office](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours") of the Roman Catholic Church. In many cities, especially in England, Anglican and Roman Catholic priests and lay people often meet several times a week to pray the office in common. A small but enthusiastic minority use the [Anglican Breviary](/wiki/Anglican_Breviary "Anglican Breviary"), or other translations and adaptations of the pre–Vatican II Roman Rite and [Sarum Rite](/wiki/Sarum_Rite "Sarum Rite"), along with supplemental material from cognate western sources, to provide such things as a common of Octaves, a common of Holy Women, and other additional material. Others may privately use idiosyncratic forms borrowed from a wide range of Christian traditions.

#### "Quires and Places where they sing"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: \"Quires and Places where they sing\"")]

Main article: [Anglican church music](/wiki/Anglican_church_music "Anglican church music")

In the late medieval period, many English cathedrals and monasteries had established small choirs of trained [lay clerks](/wiki/Lay_clerk "Lay clerk") and boy [choristers](/wiki/Choir "Choir") to perform [polyphonic](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") settings of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(music) "Mass (music)") in their [Lady chapels](/wiki/Lady_chapel "Lady chapel"). Although these "Lady Masses" were discontinued at the Reformation, the associated musical tradition was maintained in the [Elizabethan Settlement](/wiki/Elizabethan_Settlement "Elizabethan Settlement") through the establishment of choral foundations for daily singing of the Divine Office by expanded choirs of men and boys. This resulted from an explicit addition by Elizabeth herself to the injunctions accompanying the [1559 *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1559) "Book of Common Prayer (1559)") (that had itself made no mention of choral worship) by which existing choral foundations and choir schools were instructed to be continued, and their endowments secured. Consequently, some thirty-four cathedrals, collegiate churches, and royal chapels maintained paid establishments of lay singing men and choristers in the late 16th century.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMould200794-123)

All save four of these have – with interruptions during the [Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Interregnum "English Interregnum") and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") – continued daily choral prayer and praise to this day. In the Offices of Morning Prayer and Evening Prayer in the [1662 *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1662) "Book of Common Prayer (1662)"), these choral establishments are specified as "Quires and Places where they sing".

For nearly three centuries, this round of daily professional choral worship represented a tradition entirely distinct from that embodied in the intoning of [Parish Clerks](/w/index.php?title=Parish_Clerk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Parish Clerk (page does not exist)"), and the singing of "[west gallery choirs](/wiki/West_gallery_music "West gallery music")" which commonly accompanied weekly worship in English parish churches. In 1841, the rebuilt [Leeds Parish Church](/wiki/Leeds_Parish_Church "Leeds Parish Church") established a surpliced [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir") to accompany parish services, drawing explicitly on the musical traditions of the ancient choral foundations. Over the next century, the Leeds example proved immensely popular and influential for choirs in cathedrals, parish churches, and schools throughout the Anglican communion.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMould2007177-124) More or less extensively adapted, this choral tradition also became the direct inspiration for robed choirs leading congregational worship in a wide range of Christian denominations.

In 1719, the cathedral choirs of [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_Cathedral "Gloucester Cathedral"), [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford_Cathedral "Hereford Cathedral"), and [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester_Cathedral "Worcester Cathedral") combined to establish the annual [Three Choirs Festival](/wiki/Three_Choirs_Festival "Three Choirs Festival"), the precursor for the multitude of summer music festivals since. By the 20th century, the choral tradition had become for many the most accessible face of worldwide Anglicanism – especially as promoted through the regular broadcasting of choral evensong by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"); and also in the annual televising of the festival of [Nine Lessons and Carols](/wiki/Nine_Lessons_and_Carols "Nine Lessons and Carols") from [King's College, Cambridge](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge"). Composers closely concerned with this tradition include [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar"), [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams"), [Gustav Holst](/wiki/Gustav_Holst "Gustav Holst"), [Charles Villiers Stanford](/wiki/Charles_Villiers_Stanford "Charles Villiers Stanford"), and [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten"). A number of important 20th-century works by non-Anglican composers were originally commissioned for the Anglican choral tradition – for example, the *[Chichester Psalms](/wiki/Chichester_Psalms "Chichester Psalms")* of [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein") and the *[Nunc dimittis](/wiki/Nunc_dimittis "Nunc dimittis")* of [Arvo Pärt](/wiki/Arvo_P%C3%A4rt "Arvo Pärt").

Contrary to popular misconception, the British monarch is not the constitutional "head" of the Church of England but is, in law, the church's "[supreme governor](/wiki/Supreme_governor "Supreme governor")", nor does the monarch have any role in provinces outside England. The role of the crown in the Church of England is practically limited to the appointment of bishops, including the archbishop of Canterbury, and even this role is limited, as the church presents the government with a short list of candidates from which to choose. This process is accomplished through collaboration with and consent of ecclesial representatives *(see [Ecclesiastical Commissioners](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Commissioners "Ecclesiastical Commissioners"))*. Although the monarch has no constitutional role in Anglican churches in other parts of the world, the prayer books of several countries where the monarch is head of state contain prayers for him or her as sovereign.

A characteristic of Anglicanism is that it has no international juridical authority. All forty-two provinces of the Anglican Communion are autonomous, each with their own [primate](/wiki/Primate_(bishop) "Primate (bishop)") and governing structure. These provinces may take the form of national churches (such as in Canada, Uganda or Japan) or a collection of nations (such as the West Indies, Central Africa or South Asia), or geographical regions (such as Vanuatu and Solomon Islands) etc. Within these provinces there may exist subdivisions, called [ecclesiastical provinces](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province"), under the jurisdiction of a metropolitan archbishop.

All provinces of the Anglican Communion consist of [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"), each under the jurisdiction of a bishop. In the Anglican tradition, bishops must be consecrated according to the strictures of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"), which Anglicans consider one of the marks of [catholicity](/wiki/Catholicity "Catholicity"). Apart from bishops, there are two other orders of ordained ministry: [deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon") and priest.

No requirement is made for [clerical celibacy](/wiki/Clerical_celibacy "Clerical celibacy"), though many Anglo-Catholic priests have traditionally been bachelors. Because of innovations that occurred at various points after the latter half of the 20th century, women may be ordained as deacons in almost all provinces, as priests in most and as bishops in many. [Anglican religious orders](/wiki/Anglican_religious_order "Anglican religious order") and communities, suppressed in England during the Reformation, have re-emerged, especially since the mid-19th century, and now have an international presence and influence.

Government in the Anglican Communion is [synodical](/wiki/Synod "Synod"), consisting of three houses of [laity](/wiki/Laity "Laity") (usually elected parish representatives), clergy and bishops. National, provincial and diocesan synods maintain different scopes of authority, depending on their [canons and constitutions](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"). Anglicanism is not [congregational](/wiki/Congregationalist_polity "Congregationalist polity") in its polity: it is the diocese, not the parish church, which is the smallest unit of authority in the church. *(See [Episcopal polity](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity"))*.

### Archbishop of Canterbury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Archbishop of Canterbury")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Angl-Canterbury-Arms.svg/250px-Angl-Canterbury-Arms.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Angl-Canterbury-Arms.svg)

The [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of the [episcopal see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") of Canterbury

The [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") has a precedence of honour over the other primates of the Anglican Communion, and for a province to be considered a part of the communion means specifically to be in full communion with the [see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") of [Canterbury](/wiki/Province_of_Canterbury "Province of Canterbury") – though this principle is currently subject to considerable debate, especially among those in the so-called Global South, including American Anglicans.[[120]](#cite_note-125) The archbishop is, therefore, recognised as the *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")* ([lat](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). "first amongst equals") even though no direct authority in any [province](/wiki/Province_(Anglican) "Province (Anglican)") outside of England is exercised by the archbishop.[[121]](#cite_note-126)[[122]](#cite_note-127) [Rowan Williams](/wiki/Rowan_Williams "Rowan Williams"), the archbishop of Canterbury from 2002 to 2012, was the first archbishop appointed from outside the Church of England since the Reformation as he was formerly the [archbishop of Wales](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Wales "Archbishop of Wales"). On October 3, 2025, the monarch of the UK appointed [Sarah Mullally](/wiki/Sarah_Mullally "Sarah Mullally") as the archbishop of Canterbury making her the first woman to serve in the role and the first woman to be the "first among equals" and an "instrument of communion" within the Anglican Communion.[[123]](#cite_note-128)[[124]](#cite_note-129) On October 16, 2025, the Chair of GAFCON, [Laurent Mbanda](/wiki/Laurent_Mbanda "Laurent Mbanda"), the Primate of Rwanda, announced that GAFCON no longer recognises the Archbishop of Canterbury as an Instrument of Communion.[[125]](#cite_note-130)[[126]](#cite_note-131)

As spiritual head of the communion, the archbishop of Canterbury maintains a certain [moral authority](/wiki/Moral_authority "Moral authority") and has the right to determine which churches will be in communion with the [see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see"). The archbishop hosts and chairs both the [Lambeth Conferences](/wiki/Lambeth_Conference "Lambeth Conference") of Anglican Communion bishops and the [Anglican Communion Primates' Meeting](/wiki/Anglican_Communion_Primates%27_Meeting "Anglican Communion Primates' Meeting") and is responsible for the invitations to them. The archbishop also acts as the president of the secretariat of the Anglican Communion Office and its deliberative body, the [Anglican Consultative Council](/wiki/Anglican_Consultative_Council "Anglican Consultative Council").

### Conferences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Conferences")]

The Anglican Communion has no international juridical organisation. All international bodies are consultative and collaborative, and their resolutions are not legally binding on the autonomous provinces of the communion. There are three international bodies of note.

* The [Lambeth Conference](/wiki/Lambeth_Conference "Lambeth Conference") is the oldest international consultation. It was first convened by Archbishop [Charles Longley](/wiki/Charles_Longley "Charles Longley") in 1867 as a vehicle for bishops of the communion to "discuss matters of practical interest, and pronounce what we deem expedient in resolutions which may serve as safe guides to future action". Since then, it has been held roughly every ten years. Invitation is by the archbishop of Canterbury.
* The [Anglican Consultative Council](/wiki/Anglican_Consultative_Council "Anglican Consultative Council") was created by a 1968 Lambeth Conference resolution and meets [biennially](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/biennial "wikt:biennial"). The council consists of representative bishops, clergy and laity chosen by the forty-two provinces. The body has a permanent secretariat, the Anglican Communion Office, of which the archbishop of Canterbury is president.
* The [Anglican Communion Primates' Meeting](/wiki/Anglican_Communion_Primates%27_Meeting "Anglican Communion Primates' Meeting") is the most recent manifestation of international consultation and deliberation, having been first convened by Archbishop [Donald Coggan](/wiki/Donald_Coggan "Donald Coggan") in 1978 as a forum for "leisurely thought, prayer and deep consultation".[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENunley2005133-132)

### Ordained ministry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Ordained ministry")]

Further information on the Anglican priesthood: [Anglican ministry](/wiki/Anglican_ministry "Anglican ministry")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Chasublepurple.jpg/250px-Chasublepurple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chasublepurple.jpg)

An Anglican priest in Eucharistic [vestment](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment")

Like the Roman Catholic Church and the [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") churches, the Anglican Communion maintains the threefold ministry of [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), [presbyters](/wiki/Presbyter "Presbyter") (usually called "priests"), and bishops.

#### Episcopate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Episcopate")]

Main article: [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop")

Bishops, who possess the fullness of Christian priesthood, are the successors of the [apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament"). [Primates](/wiki/Primate_(bishop) "Primate (bishop)"), archbishops, and [metropolitans](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") are all bishops and members of the [historical episcopate](/wiki/Historical_episcopate "Historical episcopate") who derive their authority through [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") – an unbroken line of bishops that can be traced back to the 12 apostles of Jesus. The churches of the Anglican Communion have traditionally held that ordination in the [historic episcopate](/wiki/Historic_episcopate "Historic episcopate") is a core element in the validity of clerical ordinations. The Roman Catholic Church, however, does not recognise Anglican orders (see *[Apostolicae curae](/wiki/Apostolicae_curae "Apostolicae curae")*).[[128]](#cite_note-133) Some [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") churches have issued statements to the effect that Anglican orders could be accepted, yet have still reordained former Anglican clergy; other Eastern Orthodox churches have rejected Anglican orders altogether. In 1922, the Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople, [Meletius IV](/wiki/Meletius_Metaxakis "Meletius Metaxakis"), followed by the Synod of Constantinople, issued a statement recognising Anglican Holy Orders within the Anglican Communion as valid through apostolic succession.[[129]](#cite_note-:1-134)[[130]](#cite_note-BettensonMaunder2011-135) Following Constantinople, five other autocephalous Orthodox jurisdictions recognised the validity of Anglican Holy Orders, including [Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Church_of_Cyprus "Church of Cyprus"), [Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece") and [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church").[[131]](#cite_note-136)[[129]](#cite_note-:1-134)[[132]](#cite_note-Cox2021-137) Orthodox bishop [Kallistos Ware](/wiki/Kallistos_Ware "Kallistos Ware") explains this apparent discrepancy as follows:

> Anglican clergy who join the Orthodox Church are reordained; but [some Orthodox churches hold that] if Anglicanism and Orthodoxy were to reach full unity in the faith, perhaps such reordination might not be found necessary. It should be added, however, that a number of individual Orthodox theologians hold that under no circumstances would it be possible to recognise the validity of Anglican Orders.[[133]](#cite_note-138)

#### Priesthood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Priesthood")]

Bishops are assisted by [priests](/wiki/Episcopal_priest "Episcopal priest") and [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"). Most ordained ministers in the Anglican Communion are priests, who usually work in parishes within a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). Priests are in charge of the spiritual life of parishes and are usually called the [rector](/wiki/Rector_(ecclesiastical) "Rector (ecclesiastical)") or [vicar](/wiki/Vicar "Vicar"). A [curate](/wiki/Curate "Curate") (or, more correctly, an "assistant curate") is a priest or deacon who assists the parish priest. Non-parochial priests may earn their living by any vocation, although employment by educational institutions or charitable organisations is most common. Priests also serve as chaplains of hospitals, schools, prisons, and in the armed forces.

An [archdeacon](/wiki/Archdeacon "Archdeacon") is a priest or deacon responsible for administration of an [archdeaconry](/wiki/Archdeaconry "Archdeaconry"), which is often the name given to the principal subdivisions of a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). An archdeacon represents the diocesan bishop in his or her archdeaconry. In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), the position of archdeacon can only be held by someone in priestly orders who has been ordained for at least six years. In some other parts of the Anglican Communion, the position can also be held by deacons. In parts of the Anglican Communion where women cannot be ordained as priests or bishops but can be ordained as deacons, the position of archdeacon is effectively the most senior office to which an ordained woman can be appointed.

A [dean](/wiki/Dean_(Christianity) "Dean (Christianity)") is a priest who is the principal cleric of a cathedral or other collegiate church and the head of the chapter of canons. If the cathedral or collegiate church has its own parish, the dean is usually also rector of the parish. However, in the Church of Ireland, the roles are often separated, and most cathedrals in the Church of England do not have associated parishes. In the Church in Wales, however, most cathedrals are parish churches and their deans are now also vicars of their parishes.

The Anglican Communion recognises [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") ordinations as valid. Outside the Anglican Communion, Anglican ordinations (at least of male priests) are recognised by the [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church"), Porvoo Communion Lutherans, and various [Independent Catholic](/wiki/Independent_Catholic "Independent Catholic") churches.

#### Diaconate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Diaconate")]

Main article: [Deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Stoledeacon.jpg/250px-Stoledeacon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stoledeacon.jpg)

The vestments of a [deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), including a [stole](/wiki/Stole_(vestment) "Stole (vestment)") over the left shoulder

In Anglican churches, deacons often work directly in ministry to the marginalised inside and outside the church: the poor, the sick, the hungry, the imprisoned. Unlike Orthodox and most Roman Catholic deacons who may be married only before ordination, deacons are permitted to marry freely both before and after ordination, as are priests. Most deacons are preparing for priesthood and usually only remain as deacons for about a year before being ordained priests. However, there are some deacons who remain so.

Many provinces of the Anglican Communion ordain both men and women as deacons. Many of those provinces that ordain women to the priesthood previously allowed them to be ordained only to the diaconate. The effect of this was the creation of a large and overwhelmingly female diaconate for a time, as most men proceeded to be ordained priest after a short time as a deacon.

Deacons, in some dioceses, can be granted licences to [solemnise matrimony](/wiki/Wedding "Wedding"), usually under the instruction of their parish priest and bishop. They sometimes officiate at [Benediction of the Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Benediction_of_the_Blessed_Sacrament "Benediction of the Blessed Sacrament") in churches which have this service. Deacons are not permitted to preside at the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") (but can lead worship with the distribution of already consecrated communion where this is permitted),[[134]](#cite_note-139) [absolve sins](/wiki/Absolution_(religious) "Absolution (religious)"), or [pronounce a blessing](/wiki/Blessing "Blessing").[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoss1943393-140) It is the prohibition against deacons pronouncing blessings that leads some to believe that deacons cannot solemnise matrimony.

### Laity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Laity")]

All baptised members of the church are called Christian [faithful](/wiki/Faithful_(baptized_Catholic) "Faithful (baptized Catholic)"), truly equal in dignity and in the work to build the church. Some non-ordained people also have a formal public ministry, often on a full-time and long-term basis – such as [lay readers](/wiki/Lay_reader "Lay reader") (also known as readers), [churchwardens](/wiki/Churchwarden "Churchwarden"), [vergers](/wiki/Verger "Verger"), and [sextons](/wiki/Sexton_(office) "Sexton (office)"). Other lay positions include acolytes (male or female, often children), lay [eucharistic ministers](/wiki/Eucharistic_minister "Eucharistic minister") (also known as chalice bearers), and lay eucharistic visitors (who deliver consecrated bread and wine to "shut-ins" or members of the parish who are unable to leave home or hospital to attend the Eucharist). Lay people also serve on the parish altar guild (preparing the altar and caring for its candles, linens, flowers, etc.), in the choir and as cantors, as ushers and greeters, and on the church council (called the "vestry" in some countries), which is the governing body of a parish.

### Religious orders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Religious orders")]

See also: [Anglican religious order](/wiki/Anglican_religious_order "Anglican religious order") and [Anglican devotions](/wiki/Anglican_devotions "Anglican devotions")

A small yet influential aspect of Anglicanism is its [religious orders](/wiki/Anglican_religious_order "Anglican religious order") and communities. Shortly after the beginning of the [Catholic Revival](/wiki/Catholic_Revival "Catholic Revival") in the Church of England, there was a renewal of interest in re-establishing religious and monastic orders and communities. One of Henry VIII's earliest acts was their [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries") and seizure of their assets. In 1841, Marian Rebecca Hughes became the first woman to take the vows of religion in communion with the [Province of Canterbury](/wiki/Province_of_Canterbury "Province of Canterbury") since the Reformation.

In 1848, [Priscilla Lydia Sellon](/wiki/Priscilla_Lydia_Sellon "Priscilla Lydia Sellon") became the superior of the Society of the Most Holy Trinity at [Devonport, Plymouth](/wiki/Devonport,_Plymouth "Devonport, Plymouth"), the first organised religious order. Sellon is called "the restorer, after three centuries, of the religious life in the Church of England".[[136]](#cite_note-141) For the next one hundred years, religious orders for both men and women proliferated throughout the world, becoming a numerically small but disproportionately influential feature of global Anglicanism.

Anglican religious life at one time boasted hundreds of orders and communities, and thousands of [religious](/wiki/Religious_order "Religious order"). An important aspect of Anglican religious life is that most communities of both men and women lived their lives consecrated to God under the [vows](/wiki/Vow "Vow") of [poverty, chastity, and obedience](/wiki/Poverty,_chastity,_and_obedience "Poverty, chastity, and obedience"), or, in [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") communities, Stability, Conversion of Life, and Obedience, by practising a mixed life of reciting the full eight services of the [Breviary](/wiki/Breviary "Breviary") in choir, along with a daily [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), plus service to the poor. The mixed life, combining aspects of the contemplative orders and the active orders, remains to this day a hallmark of Anglican religious life. Another distinctive feature of Anglican religious life is the existence of some mixed-gender communities.

Since the 1960s, there has been a sharp decline in the number of professed religious in most parts of the Anglican Communion, especially in North America, Europe, and Australia. Many once large and international communities have been reduced to a single [convent](/wiki/Convent "Convent") or [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") with memberships of elderly men or women. In the last few decades of the 20th century, novices have for most communities been few and far between. Some orders and communities have already become extinct. There are, however, still thousands of Anglican religious working today in approximately 200 communities around the world, and religious life in many parts of the Communion – especially in developing nations – flourishes.

The most significant growth has been in the [Melanesian](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia") countries of the [Solomon Islands](/wiki/Solomon_Islands "Solomon Islands"), [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu"), and [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"). The [Melanesian Brotherhood](/wiki/Melanesian_Brotherhood "Melanesian Brotherhood"), founded at [Tabalia](/wiki/Tabalia "Tabalia"), [Guadalcanal](/wiki/Guadalcanal_(Pacific_Ocean_island) "Guadalcanal (Pacific Ocean island)"), in 1925 by Ini Kopuria, is now the largest Anglican Community in the world, with over 450 [brothers](/wiki/Monk "Monk") in the Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, Papua New Guinea, the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), and the United Kingdom. The [Sisters of the Church](/wiki/Sisters_of_the_Church "Sisters of the Church"), started by Mother [Emily Ayckbowm](/wiki/Emily_Ayckbowm "Emily Ayckbowm") in England in 1870, has more [sisters](/wiki/Nun "Nun") in the Solomons than all their other communities. The [Community of the Sisters of Melanesia](/wiki/Community_of_the_Sisters_of_Melanesia "Community of the Sisters of Melanesia"), started in 1980 by Sister Nesta Tiboe, is a growing community of women in the Solomon Islands.

The [Society of Saint Francis](/wiki/Society_of_Saint_Francis "Society of Saint Francis"), founded as a union of various [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") orders in the 1920s, has experienced great growth in the Solomon Islands. Other communities of religious have been started by Anglicans in Papua New Guinea and in Vanuatu. Most Melanesian Anglican religious are in their early to mid-20s. Vows may be temporary, and it is generally assumed that brothers, at least, will leave and marry in due course, making the average age 40 to 50 years younger than their brothers and sisters in other countries. Growth of religious orders, especially for women, is marked in certain parts of Africa.

### Worldwide distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Worldwide distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Anglican_C.%2C_Porvoo_C.%2C_Utrecht_Union.svg/960px-Anglican_C.%2C_Porvoo_C.%2C_Utrecht_Union.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Anglican_C.,_Porvoo_C.,_Utrecht_Union.svg)

A world map showing the provinces of the **Anglican Communion** (blue). Shown are the Churches in full communion with the Anglican Church: The Nordic Lutheran churches of the [Porvoo Communion](/wiki/Porvoo_Communion "Porvoo Communion") (green), and the [Old Catholic Churches](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church") in the [Utrecht Union](/wiki/Utrecht_Union "Utrecht Union") (red).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Jamestown_Virginia_ruin.JPG/250px-Jamestown_Virginia_ruin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jamestown_Virginia_ruin.JPG)

An 1854 image of the ruins of [Jamestown Church](/wiki/Jamestown_Church "Jamestown Church") in [Jamestown, Virginia](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia"), the first Anglican church in North America

As of 2024, there are around 110 million Anglicans worldwide.[[4]](#cite_note-McKeown-4) Of these, as of 2020, around 95 million are members of the Anglican Communion (excluding [united churches](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches")).[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)[[27]](#cite_note-:3-27)
This makes the Anglican Communion the third largest Christian communion in the world, after the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorsley2015-10)

The 11 provinces in Africa saw growth in the last two decades. The [World Christian Database](/wiki/World_Christian_Encyclopedia "World Christian Encyclopedia"), published in 2021 and produced by the Center for the Study of Global Christianity at [Gordon-Conwell Theological Seminary](/wiki/Gordon-Conwell_Theological_Seminary "Gordon-Conwell Theological Seminary"), and the [World Christian Encyclopedia](/wiki/World_Christian_Encyclopedia "World Christian Encyclopedia"), published by [*Edinburgh University Press*](/wiki/Edinburgh_University_Press "Edinburgh University Press"), estimated that the Anglican Communion had 97,399,000 members in 2020 with 63,556,000 in Africa, 24,400,000 in Europe, 4,565,000 in Oceania, 2,689,000 in Northern America, 1,230,000 in Asia, and 959,000 in Latin America (excluding the United churches).[[137]](#cite_note-142)[[28]](#cite_note-:4-28) The ten largest member churches of the Anglican Communion, as measured by total reported membership not active membership, in 2025 were the churches based in [England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Church_of_Nigeria "Church of Nigeria"), [Uganda](/wiki/Church_of_Uganda "Church of Uganda"), [Kenya](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Kenya "Anglican Church of Kenya"), [Australia](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Australia "Anglican Church of Australia"), [South India](/wiki/Church_of_South_India "Church of South India"), [Southern Africa](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Southern_Africa "Anglican Church of Southern Africa"), [South Sudan](/wiki/Province_of_the_Episcopal_Church_of_South_Sudan "Province of the Episcopal Church of South Sudan"), [Tanzania](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Tanzania "Anglican Church of Tanzania"), and the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)").[[138]](#cite_note-143) In most industrialised countries, church attendance has decreased since the 19th century. Anglicanism's presence in the rest of the world is due to large-scale emigration, the establishment of expatriate communities, or the work of missionaries.

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") has been a church of [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") since the 17th century, when the Church first left English shores with colonists who founded what would become the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, and South Africa, and established Anglican churches. For example, an Anglican chaplain, [Robert Wolfall](/wiki/Robert_Wolfall "Robert Wolfall"), with [Martin Frobisher](/wiki/Martin_Frobisher "Martin Frobisher")'s [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") expedition, celebrated the Eucharist in 1578 in [Frobisher Bay](/wiki/Frobisher_Bay "Frobisher Bay").

The first Anglican church in the Americas was built at [Jamestown, Virginia](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia"), in 1607. By the 18th century, missionaries worked to establish Anglican churches in Asia, Africa, and Latin America. The great Church of England missionary societies were founded; for example, the [Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge](/wiki/Society_for_Promoting_Christian_Knowledge "Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge") (SPCK) in 1698, the [Society for the Propagation of the Gospel in Foreign Parts](/wiki/Society_for_the_Propagation_of_the_Gospel_in_Foreign_Parts "Society for the Propagation of the Gospel in Foreign Parts") (SPG) in 1701, and the [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society") (CMS) in 1799. In the 19th century, social-oriented evangelism with societies were founded and developed, including the [Church Pastoral Aid Society](/wiki/Church_Pastoral_Aid_Society "Church Pastoral Aid Society") (CPAS) in 1836, [Mission to Seafarers](/wiki/Mission_to_Seafarers "Mission to Seafarers") in 1856, [Girls' Friendly Society](/wiki/Girls%27_Friendly_Society "Girls' Friendly Society") (GFS) in 1875, [Mothers' Union](/wiki/Mothers%27_Union "Mothers' Union") in 1876, and [Church Army](/wiki/Church_Army "Church Army") in 1882, all carrying out a personal form of evangelism.

In the 20th century, the Church of England developed new forms of evangelism, including the [Alpha course](/wiki/Alpha_course "Alpha course") in 1990, which was developed and propagated from [Holy Trinity Brompton Church](/wiki/Holy_Trinity_Brompton_Church "Holy Trinity Brompton Church") in London. In the 21st century, there has been renewed effort to reach children and youth. [Fresh expressions](/wiki/Fresh_expressions "Fresh expressions") is a Church of England missionary initiative to youth begun in 2005, and has ministries at a [skate park](/wiki/Skate_park "Skate park")[[139]](#cite_note-144) through the efforts of St George's Church, [Benfleet](/wiki/Benfleet_Urban_District "Benfleet Urban District"), Essex, the [Diocese of Chelmsford](/wiki/Diocese_of_Chelmsford "Diocese of Chelmsford"), or youth groups with evocative names, like the C.L.A.W (Christ Little Angels – Whatever!) youth group at [Coventry Cathedral](/wiki/Coventry_Cathedral "Coventry Cathedral"). For those who prefer not to actually visit a [brick and mortar](/wiki/Brick_and_mortar "Brick and mortar") church, there are Internet ministries, such as the [Diocese of Oxford](/wiki/Diocese_of_Oxford "Diocese of Oxford")'s online Anglican i-Church, which was founded on the web in 2005.

### Ecumenism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Ecumenism")]

Further information on the ongoing dialogue between Anglicanism and the wider Church: [Anglican communion and ecumenism](/wiki/Anglican_communion_and_ecumenism "Anglican communion and ecumenism")

Anglican interest in [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") dialogue can be traced back to the time of the Reformation and dialogues with both Orthodox and Lutheran churches in the 16th century. In the 19th century, with the rise of the Oxford Movement, there arose greater concern for reunion of the churches of "Catholic confession". This desire to work towards full [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christian) "Communion (Christian)") with other denominations led to the development of the [Chicago-Lambeth Quadrilateral](/wiki/Chicago-Lambeth_Quadrilateral "Chicago-Lambeth Quadrilateral"), approved by the third [Lambeth Conference](/wiki/Lambeth_Conference "Lambeth Conference") of 1888. The four points (the sufficiency of scripture, the historic creeds, the two dominical sacraments, and the historic episcopate) were proposed as a basis for discussion, although they have frequently been taken as a non-negotiable bottom-line for any form of reunion.

### Theological diversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Theological diversity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/High_Altar%2C_Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_%28Rosemont%2C_Pennsylvania%29.jpg/250px-High_Altar%2C_Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_%28Rosemont%2C_Pennsylvania%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Altar,_Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_(Rosemont,_Pennsylvania).jpg)

The high [altar](/wiki/Altar "Altar") at the Anglo-Catholic [Church of the Good Shepherd](/wiki/Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_(Rosemont,_Pennsylvania) "Church of the Good Shepherd (Rosemont, Pennsylvania)") in [Rosemont, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Rosemont,_Pennsylvania "Rosemont, Pennsylvania")

Anglicanism in general has always sought a balance between the emphases of [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") and [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), while tolerating a range of expressions of [evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") and ceremony. Clergy and laity from all Anglican [churchmanship](/wiki/Churchmanship "Churchmanship") traditions have been active in the formation of the Continuing movement.

While there are [high church](/wiki/High_church "High church"), [broad-church](/wiki/Broad_church "Broad church") and [low-church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") Continuing Anglicans, many Continuing churches are [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") with highly ceremonial liturgical practices. Others belong to a more evangelical or [low-church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") tradition and tend to support the [Thirty-nine Articles](/wiki/Thirty-nine_Articles "Thirty-nine Articles") and simpler worship services. [Morning Prayer](/wiki/Morning_Prayer_(Anglican) "Morning Prayer (Anglican)"), for instance, is often used instead of the [Holy Eucharist](/wiki/Holy_Eucharist "Holy Eucharist") for Sunday worship services, although this is not necessarily true of all low-church parishes.

Most Continuing churches in the United States reject the 1979 revision of the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* by the Episcopal Church and use the 1928 version for their services instead. In addition, Anglo-Catholic bodies may use the [Anglican Missal](/wiki/Anglican_Missal "Anglican Missal"), [Anglican Service Book](/wiki/Anglican_Service_Book "Anglican Service Book") or [English Missal](/wiki/English_Missal "English Missal") when celebrating Mass.

#### Internal conflict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Internal conflict")]

A changing focus on social issues after the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") led to Lambeth Conference resolutions countenancing [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception") and the [remarriage](/wiki/Remarriage "Remarriage") of divorced persons. Eventually, most provinces approved the [ordination of women](/wiki/Ordination_of_women "Ordination of women"). In more recent years, some jurisdictions have permitted the ordination of people in same-sex relationships and authorised rites for the blessing of same-sex unions (see [Homosexuality and Anglicanism](/wiki/Homosexuality_and_Anglicanism "Homosexuality and Anglicanism")). "The more liberal provinces that are open to changing Church doctrine on marriage in order to allow for same-sex unions include Brazil, Canada, New Zealand, Scotland, South India, South Africa, the US and Wales",[[142]](#cite_note-147)[[143]](#cite_note-148) while the more conservative provinces are primarily located in the Global South.

The lack of social consensus among and within provinces of diverse cultural traditions has resulted in considerable conflict and even schism concerning some or all of these developments, as was the case in the [Anglican realignment](/wiki/Anglican_realignment "Anglican realignment"). More conservative elements within and outside of Anglicanism (primarily African churches and factions within North American Anglicanism) have opposed these changes,[[144]](#cite_note-149) while some liberal and moderate Anglicans see this opposition as representing a new [fundamentalism](/wiki/Fundamentalism "Fundamentalism") within Anglicanism and "believe a split is inevitable and preferable to continued infighting and paralysis."[[145]](#cite_note-150) Some Anglicans opposed to various liberalising changes, in particular the ordination of women, have become Roman Catholics or Orthodox. Others have, at various times, joined the [Continuing Anglican movement](/wiki/Continuing_Anglican_movement "Continuing Anglican movement") or departed for non-Anglican evangelical churches.

## Continuum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Continuum")]

Main article: [Continuing Anglican movement](/wiki/Continuing_Anglican_movement "Continuing Anglican movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Lincoln_Cathedral_West_Front_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7562875.jpg/250px-Lincoln_Cathedral_West_Front_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7562875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_Cathedral_West_Front_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7562875.jpg)

[Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral "Lincoln Cathedral")

The term "[Continuing Anglicanism](/wiki/Continuing_Anglicanism "Continuing Anglicanism")" refers to a number of church bodies which have formed outside of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") in the belief that traditional forms of Anglican faith, worship, and order have been unacceptably revised or abandoned within some Anglican Communion churches in recent decades. They therefore claim that they are "continuing" traditional Anglicanism.

The modern Continuing Anglican movement principally dates to the [Congress of St. Louis](/wiki/Congress_of_St._Louis "Congress of St. Louis"), held in the United States in 1977, where participants rejected changes that had been made in the Episcopal Church's *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* and also the Episcopal Church's approval of the [ordination of women](/wiki/Ordination_of_women "Ordination of women") to the priesthood. More recent changes in the North American churches of the Anglican Communion, such as the introduction of same-sex marriage rites and the ordination of gay and lesbian people to the [priesthood](/wiki/Priesthood "Priesthood") and [episcopate](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"), have created further separations.

Continuing churches have generally been formed by people who have left the Anglican Communion. The original Anglican churches are charged by the Continuing Anglicans with being greatly compromised by secular cultural standards and liberal theology. Many Continuing Anglicans believe that the faith of some churches in communion with the archbishop of Canterbury has become [unorthodox](/wiki/Heterodox "Heterodox") and therefore have not sought to also be in communion with him. The original continuing parishes in the United States were found mainly in metropolitan areas. Since the late 1990s, a number have appeared in smaller communities, often as a result of a division in the town's existing Episcopal churches. The 2007–08 *Directory of Traditional Anglican and Episcopal Parishes*, published by the Fellowship of Concerned Churchmen, contained information on over 900 parishes affiliated with either the Continuing Anglican churches or the Anglican realignment movement, a more recent wave of Anglicans withdrawing from the Anglican Communion's North American provinces.

## Social activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Social activism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Christchurch_Cathedral_-_Dublin.jpg/250px-Christchurch_Cathedral_-_Dublin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christchurch_Cathedral_-_Dublin.jpg)

[Christ Church Cathedral](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral,_Dublin "Christ Church Cathedral, Dublin") in Dublin, Ireland

A concern for [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") can be traced to very early Anglican beliefs, relating to an intertwined theology of God, nature, and humanity. The Anglican theologian [Richard Hooker](/wiki/Richard_Hooker "Richard Hooker") wrote in his book *The Works of that Learned and Judicious Divine* that "God hath created nothing simply for itself, but each thing in all things, and of every thing each part in other have such interest, that in the whole world nothing is found whereunto any thing created can say, 'I need thee not.'"[[146]](#cite_note-151) Such statements demonstrate a theological Anglican interest in social activism, which has historically appeared in movements such as evangelical Anglican [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce")'s campaign against slavery in the 18th century, or 19th century issues concerning industrialisation.[[147]](#cite_note-:0-152)

Lord Shaftesbury, a devout evangelical, campaigned to improve the conditions in factories, in mines, for chimney sweeps, and for the education of the very poor. For years, he was chairman of the [Ragged School](/wiki/Ragged_school "Ragged school") Board.[[148]](#cite_note-153) [Frederick Denison Maurice](/wiki/Frederick_Denison_Maurice "Frederick Denison Maurice") was a leading figure advocating reform, founding so-called "producer's co-operatives" and the [Working Men's College](/wiki/Working_Men%27s_College "Working Men's College"). His work was instrumental in the establishment of the [Christian socialist](/wiki/Christian_socialism "Christian socialism") movement, although he himself was not in any real sense a socialist but "a Tory paternalist with the unusual desire to theorize his acceptance of the traditional obligation to help the poor",[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1976171–172-154) influenced Anglo-Catholics such as Charles Gore, who wrote that "the principle of the incarnation is denied unless the Christian spirit can be allowed to concern itself with everything that interests and touches human life." Anglican focus on labour issues culminated in the work of [William Temple](/wiki/William_Temple_(archbishop) "William Temple (archbishop)") in the 1930s and 1940s."[[147]](#cite_note-:0-152)

### Pacifism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Pacifism")]

A question of whether or not Christianity is a [pacifist](/wiki/Pacifist "Pacifist") religion has remained a matter of debate for Anglicans. The leading Anglican spokesman for pacifist ideas, from 1914 to 1945, was [Ernest Barnes](/wiki/Ernest_Barnes "Ernest Barnes"), bishop of Birmingham from 1924 to 1953. He opposed both world wars.[[150]](#cite_note-155) In 1937, the [Anglican Pacifist Fellowship](/wiki/Anglican_Pacifist_Fellowship "Anglican Pacifist Fellowship") emerged as a distinct reform organisation, seeking to make pacifism a clearly defined part of Anglican theology. The group rapidly gained popularity amongst Anglican intellectuals, including [Vera Brittain](/wiki/Vera_Brittain "Vera Brittain"), [Evelyn Underhill](/wiki/Evelyn_Underhill "Evelyn Underhill"), and the former British political leader [George Lansbury](/wiki/George_Lansbury "George Lansbury"). Furthermore, [Dick Sheppard](/wiki/Dick_Sheppard_(priest) "Dick Sheppard (priest)"), who during the 1930s was one of Britain's most famous Anglican priests due to his landmark sermon broadcasts for [BBC Radio](/wiki/BBC_Radio "BBC Radio"), founded the [Peace Pledge Union](/wiki/Peace_Pledge_Union "Peace Pledge Union"), a secular pacifist organisation for the non-religious that gained considerable support throughout the 1930s.[[151]](#cite_note-156)

Whilst never actively endorsed by Anglican churches, many Anglicans unofficially have adopted the Augustinian "[Just War](/wiki/Just_War "Just War")" doctrine.[[152]](#cite_note-157)[[153]](#cite_note-158) The Anglican Pacifist Fellowship remains highly active throughout the Anglican world. It rejects this doctrine of "just war" and seeks to reform the Church by reintroducing the pacifism inherent in the beliefs of many of the earliest Christians and present in their interpretation of Christ's [Sermon on the Mount](/wiki/Sermon_on_the_Mount "Sermon on the Mount"). The principles of the Anglican Pacifist Fellowship are often formulated as a statement of belief that "Jesus' teaching is incompatible with the waging of war ... that a Christian church should never support or justify war ... [and] that our Christian witness should include opposing the waging or justifying of war."[[154]](#cite_note-159)

Confusing the matter was that the 37th Article of Religion in the *Book of Common Prayer* states that "it is lawful for Christian men, at the commandment of the Magistrate, to wear weapons, and serve in the wars." Therefore, the Lambeth Council in the modern era has sought to provide a clearer position by repudiating modern war and developed a statement that has been affirmed at each subsequent meeting of the council.

This statement was strongly reasserted when "the 67th General Convention of the Episcopal Church reaffirms the statement made by the Anglican Bishops assembled at Lambeth in 1978 and adopted by the 66th General Convention of the Episcopal Church in 1979, calling "Christian people everywhere ... to engage themselves in non-violent action for justice and peace and to support others so engaged, recognising that such action will be controversial and may be personally very costly... this General Convention, in obedience to this call, urges all members of this Church to support by prayer and by such other means as they deem appropriate, those who engaged in such non-violent action, and particularly those who suffer for conscience' sake as a result; and be it further Resolved, that this General Convention calls upon all members of this Church seriously to consider the implications for their own lives of this call to resist war and work for peace for their own lives."

### Opposition to apartheid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Opposition to apartheid")]

The focus on other social issues became increasingly diffuse after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The growing independence and strength of Anglican churches in the Global South brought new emphasis to issues of global poverty, the inequitable distribution of resources, and the lingering effects of colonialism. In this regard, figures such as [Desmond Tutu](/wiki/Desmond_Tutu "Desmond Tutu") and [Ted Scott](/wiki/Ted_Scott "Ted Scott") were instrumental in mobilising Anglicans worldwide against the [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") policies of South Africa.

### Abortion and euthanasia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Abortion and euthanasia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/M4L_Elvert_Barnes_IMG_2207_%2816360279245%29.jpg/250px-M4L_Elvert_Barnes_IMG_2207_%2816360279245%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:M4L_Elvert_Barnes_IMG_2207_(16360279245).jpg)

An Anglican clergyman marches with [Anglicans for Life](/wiki/Anglicans_for_Life "Anglicans for Life") at the 2015 March for Life in Washington, D.C.

While individual Anglicans and member churches within the Communion differ in practice over the circumstances in which abortion should or should not be permitted, [Lambeth Conference](/wiki/Lambeth_Conference "Lambeth Conference") resolutions have consistently held to a conservative view on the issue. The 1930 Conference, the first to be held since the initial legalisation of abortion in Europe (in [Russia](/wiki/Abortion_in_Russia "Abortion in Russia") in 1920), stated:[[155]](#cite_note-160) "The Conference further records its abhorrence of the sinful practice of abortion."

The 1958 Conference's *Family in Contemporary Society* report affirmed the following position on abortion[[156]](#cite_note-161) and was commended by the 1968 Conference:[[157]](#cite_note-162)

> In the strongest terms Christians reject the practice of induced abortion or infanticide, which involves the killing of a life already conceived (as well as a violation of the personality of the mother), save at the dictate of strict and undeniable medical necessity ... the sacredness of life is, in Christian eyes, an absolute which should not be violated.

The subsequent Lambeth Conference, in 1978, made no change to this position and commended the need for "programmes at diocesan level, involving both men and women ... to emphasise the sacredness of all human life, the moral issues inherent in clinical abortion, and the possible implications of genetic engineering."[[158]](#cite_note-163)

In the context of debates around and proposals for the legalisation of [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia "Euthanasia") and [assisted suicide](/wiki/Assisted_suicide "Assisted suicide"), the 1998 Conference affirmed that "life is God-given and has intrinsic sanctity, significance and worth".[[159]](#cite_note-1998life-164)

## Ordinariates within the Roman Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anglicanism&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Ordinariates within the Roman Catholic Church")]

On 4 November 2009, [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") issued an [apostolic constitution](/wiki/Apostolic_constitution "Apostolic constitution"), *[Anglicanorum Coetibus](/wiki/Anglicanorum_Coetibus "Anglicanorum Coetibus")*, to allow groups of former Anglicans to enter into [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") as members of [personal ordinariates](/wiki/Personal_ordinariate "Personal ordinariate").[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2011-165) 20 October 2009 announcement of the imminent constitution mentioned:[[161]](#cite_note-166)

> Today's announcement of the Apostolic Constitution is a response by Pope Benedict XVI to a number of requests over the past few years to the Holy See from groups of Anglicans who wish to enter into full visible communion with the Roman Catholic Church, and are willing to declare that they share a common Catholic faith and accept the Petrine ministry as willed by Christ for his Church.

Pope Benedict XVI approved, within the apostolic constitution, a canonical structure that provides for personal ordinariates which will allow former Anglicans to enter full communion with the Roman Catholic Church while preserving elements of distinctive Anglican spiritual patrimony.

For each personal ordinariate, the [ordinary](/wiki/Ordinary_(officer) "Ordinary (officer)") may be a former Anglican bishop or priest. It was expected that provision would be made to allow the [retention of aspects of Anglican liturgy](/wiki/Anglican_Use "Anglican Use").[[162]](#cite_note-167)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** An early occurrence is found in the [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"), which contains the sentence *Anglicana ecclesie libera sit* "the English Church shall be free".[[30]](#cite_note-30)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** According to John Godfrey,
   > The most famous and beautiful legend of all related to the conversion of Britain is of course that of Joseph of Arimathea, who is said to have arrived in Britain with twelve companions in the year 63 at the bidding of the apostle Philip. According to this legend, Joseph brought with him the Holy Grail and built, at Glastonbury, the first British church.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGodfrey19629-38)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** [John Carey](/wiki/John_Carey_(Celticist) "John Carey (Celticist)") writes that
   > 'Celtic Christianity' is a phrase used, with varying degrees of specificity, to designate a complex of features held to have been common to the Celtic-speaking countries in the early Middle Ages. Doubts concerning the term's usefulness have repeatedly been expressed, however, and the majority of scholars consider it to be problematic ...
   > While there is considerable evidence for divergent Irish and (to an even greater degree) British practice in matters of liturgy, baptism, and ecclesiastical administration, the usages in question seem only to have characterized specific regions, and not necessarily to have been uniformly present there. Only the Britons were accused of practising a heterodox baptism; traces of an archaic liturgy in Wales find no counterpart in the eclectic, but largely Gallican, worship attested from Ireland; and the superiority of abbots to bishops appears to have been limited to some parts of Gaelic sphere of influence.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarey2006431,_433-44)

   In *The Celtic Resource Book*, [Martin Wallace](/wiki/Martin_Wallace_(bishop) "Martin Wallace (bishop)") writes that
   > it is important to remember that there was never any such thing as 'The Celtic Church'. It was never an organized system in the way that we understand churches today. Rather, each Celtic church was highly independent and if there was a relationship between any of them the relationship tended to be one of spiritual support through missionary endeavour, rather than through any particular church structure. It is also important to remember that the Celtic church life as it emerged in fifth-century Ireland would be quite different to that which emerged in nineteenth century Hebridean communities. Even on the mainland the patterns of church life would vary considerably from one place to another, and from one age to another.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace20099-45)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-83)** For a study stressing the hegemony of continental Calvinism before the 1620s, see [Tyacke 1987](#CITEREFTyacke1987). For a study perceiving an emerging self-conscious "Prayer Book Episcopalism" distinct from, but a predecessor to, [Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") Anglicanism, see [Maltby 1998](#CITEREFMaltby1998).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-110)** The 19th-century evangelical interpretation of the Prayerbook, now less frequent, included celebration of Holy Communion while the priest was standing at the northern *short side* of the communion table. This misinterpretation was caused by the fact that the 1662 *Book of Common Prayer* retained two contradictory rubrics. From 1552 a rubric was retained that the priest should stand at the northern *long side* of a communion table standing east-west in the choir (the communicants sitting in the choir stalls by the northern and southern walls). From 1559 was retained the rubric that 'the chancels shall remain as they have done in times past', originally intended to protect the mediaeval interior of church buildings from Calvinist vandalism, and – mainly neglected during the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I – it was not consented to generally before the reign of Charles II. During the reign of Elizabeth I, only the [chapels royal](/wiki/Chapel_royal "Chapel royal") retained the mediaeval position of the communion table, standing permanently north-south at the east wall of the choir. The parish of St. Giles Cripplegate, London, began to apply the Chapels Royal arrangement of the communion table in 1599 or 1605, and from there it began to spread. Archbishop [William Laud](/wiki/William_Laud "William Laud")'s attempt to make it mandatory in the 1630s backfired, with well known consequences. By the reign of Charles II, however, it was applied generally, and the original intention of the *northward position rubric* became unintelligible, and easily misunderstood.

The difference was sometimes so much as a whole month between the Celtic and the Catholic Easter. When the two Churches came into contact, as they did in the North of England, this discrepancy gave rise to scandal and controversy."
